> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act) Cycle** is a systematic, iterative, four-stage quality improvement framework used to test changes in real-world settings by planning a change, trying it, observing the results, and acting on what is learned. In the KTU 2024 TQM syllabus, the PDSA cycle is positioned as the **foundational scientific method of continuous improvement**, forming the operational engine of Kaizen and the broader Deming philosophy of management.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The PDSA cycle is sometimes referred to as the **Deming Wheel**, **Deming Cycle**, or **Shewhart Cycle**. It is the *theoretical engine* under which tools like the 5 Whys, Pareto Analysis, Cause-and-Effect Diagrams, and Control Charts operate.

The cycle is formally expressed as:

$$
\boxed{\text{PDSA} \equiv \{\,\text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Study} \rightarrow \text{Act}\,\}_{\text{iterative}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** The PDSA cycle is fundamentally a *hypothesis-testing loop*. Every rotation generates empirical data that either validates, refines, or rejects the current improvement hypothesis. The iteration count $N \geq 1$ depends on the convergence of measured outcomes toward the target Key Performance Indicator (KPI).

## 1.2 Historical Lineage (Origin & Evolution)

| Era | Contributor | Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| **1920s** | **Walter A. Shewhart** | Originated the iterative learning cycle in Bell Labs |
| **1950s** | **W. Edwards Deming** | Popularized and renamed the cycle (PDSA) in post-WWII Japan |
| **1980s** | **Kaizen Institute** | Integrated PDSA into continuous improvement systems globally |
| **2000s** | **Institute for Healthcare Improvement (IHI)** | Standardized PDSA in healthcare and education |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The GPS Recalibration Loop

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are driving from your home in Kerala to a hill station like Munnar using Google Maps.
> - **PLAN:** You set the destination and study the suggested route.
> - **DO:** You start driving along the route.
> - **STUDY:** You monitor your actual position, time taken, and traffic congestion.
> - **ACT:** If the route is blocked, you accept the rerouting suggestion (and *standardize* it if it is faster) or return to the previous route.
>
> The GPS *never* stops recalibrating; it loops continuously. PDSA behaves identically — it is the **GPS of organizational improvement**.

## 1.4 Why PDSA? — The Logical Justification

Most organizational changes fail because they are:
1. Implemented at full scale without pilot testing.
2. Never scientifically evaluated post-implementation.
3. One-time events with no iterative learning mechanism.

PDSA solves all three through its **built-in test-and-learn architecture**. It is explicitly demanded by the KTU 2024 TQM syllabus as the operational mechanism for executing the **Deming Philosophy (System, Variation, Theory of Knowledge, Psychology)**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Heuristic:** When an exam question uses the word *"systematically"*, *"iteratively"*, or *"scientifically test a change"*, it is *always* signalling the **PDSA cycle** as the expected answer framework.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Phases — Operational Logic Breakdown

The PDSA cycle is not a checklist; it is a **recursive learning engine**. Each phase has a specific cognitive role mapped directly to the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (RBT)** cognitive levels mandated by the KTU 2024 OBE framework.

### 2.1.1 Phase 1 — PLAN (RBT Level: Apply / Analyze)

The planning phase converts a problem into a **falsifiable hypothesis**. It is the most documentation-intensive phase.

**Core Activities:**
- Define the problem statement using the **5W2H** framework.
- Identify the root cause via tools: *Pareto Chart, Fishbone Diagram, 5 Whys, Scatter Diagram*.
- Formulate a measurable objective with a **SMART** target.
- Predict expected outcomes and select Key Performance Indicators (KPIs).
- Design the intervention and define the pilot scope, duration, and resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **SMART Objectives** (mandated by KTU module outcomes): **S**pecific, **M**easurable, **A**chievable, **R**elevant, **T**ime-bound.

### 2.1.2 Phase 2 — DO (RBT Level: Apply)

The execution phase carries out the plan on a **small, controlled scale** to limit risk.

**Core Activities:**
- Document the process baseline before intervention.
- Execute the pilot with rigorous data collection.
- Log anomalies, deviations, and unexpected observations.
- Train the team on data-collection protocols.
- Capture both quantitative and qualitative feedback.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** The "Do" phase is *not* full-scale implementation. KTU examiners explicitly penalize answers that confuse pilot testing with enterprise-wide rollout.

### 2.1.3 Phase 3 — STUDY (RBT Level: Evaluate / Analyze)

The analytical phase converts raw observations into **evidence-based conclusions**.

**Core Activities:**
- Compare observed results against the predicted KPIs.
- Use **Control Charts** to distinguish common-cause vs special-cause variation.
- Quantify the effect using **Process Capability Indices** ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$).
- Identify gaps, side-effects, and confounding variables.
- Validate or reject the original hypothesis.

### 2.1.4 Phase 4 — ACT (RBT Level: Create / Evaluate)

The consolidation phase locks in the learning and propagates it.

**Three Possible Decisions in the ACT Phase:**

$$
\text{ACT Decision} =
\begin{cases}
\text{ADOPT} & \text{if hypothesis validated} \rightarrow \text{standardize, train, scale up} \\
\text{ADAPT} & \text{if partially validated} \rightarrow \text{modify plan, run new PDSA cycle} \\
\text{ABANDON} & \text{if hypothesis rejected} \rightarrow \text{redefine problem, restart cycle}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Many students terminate the cycle after the "Act" phase. KTU examiners specifically reward answers that emphasize the *recursion* — the next cycle begins with new knowledge from the previous one.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Tool Cheat Sheet

The table below is a **board-examination-ready reference** for the PDSA-related formulas, metrics, and tools a student is expected to recall and apply.

| Element | Formula / Tool | Application in PDSA | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Process Capability Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Used in STUDY to measure inherent process capability | dimensionless |
| **Process Capability Index (shifted)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Used in STUDY to measure actual capability after centering | dimensionless |
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O}$ | STUDY-phase defect quantification | defects per million |
| **Sigma Level Conversion** | $\sigma \approx \dfrac{1}{C_{pk}} \times 1.5 + 1.5$ | Maps capability to Six Sigma scale | $\sigma$ |
| **Pareto 80/20 Rule** | Cumulative \% defects vs. cumulative \% causes | PLAN-phase root-cause prioritization | percentage |
| **5W2H Framework** | What, Why, Where, When, Who, How, How much | PLAN-phase problem framing | qualitative |
| **Control Chart Limits** | $\text{UCL} = \bar{X} + 3\sigma \quad ; \quad \text{LCL} = \bar{X} - 3\sigma$ | STUDY-phase variation classification | variable-dependent |
| **Cycle Time Reduction** | $\Delta T = T_{\text{before}} - T_{\text{after}}$ | DO/STUDY comparative metric | time unit |
| **First-Time-Right Rate** | $\text{FTR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{passed first time}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | DO-phase execution quality | percent |
| **Yield Rate** | $Y = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times \cdots \times Y_n$ | STUDY-phase rolled throughput yield | percent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** $\mu$ = process mean, $\sigma$ = standard deviation, USL/LSL = upper/lower specification limits, $D$ = defects, $N$ = units, $O$ = opportunities per unit.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | PDSA Application |
|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | Reducing line rejection rate from **8% to 2%** in an automotive plant |
| **Software (KTU CS Branch)** | Iterative sprint-level quality improvement in Agile/Scrum |
| **Healthcare** | Reducing hospital-acquired infections through hand-hygiene pilots |
| **Civil Engineering** | Optimizing concrete mix-design curing time |
| **Education (NEP 2020)** | Improving student learning outcomes at the classroom level |

## 2.4 PDSA vs PDCA — The KTU-Favoured Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Watch:** Many textbooks (especially older ones) call this the PDCA cycle. Modern Deming literature prefers **PDSA** because "Study" implies rigorous analysis, while "Check" implies mere verification.

| Dimension | PDSA (Deming's preferred) | PDCA (older usage) |
|---|---|---|
| Originator's intent | Deep analytical study | Surface-level verification |
| Knowledge outcome | Theory-building | Status confirmation |
| KTU 2024 syllabus term | **PDSA** | Not preferred |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Decision Matrix

This section provides an **exhaustive, line-by-line analytical derivation** of how the PDSA cycle is operationalized in a real TQM project. Each phase is decomposed into its constituent sub-steps, decision rules, and quantitative checkpoints. The framework is intentionally over-specified because KTU 14-mark questions routinely demand this level of granularity.

## 3.1 The 5W2H Decomposition (Mandatory in PLAN Phase)

The PLAN phase cannot begin without a fully resolved 5W2H grid. Below is the explicit derivation of each element.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PLAN} &\equiv \Big\{\, W_1, W_2, W_3, W_4, W_5, H_1, H_2 \,\Big\} \\[4pt]
W_1 &: \text{What is the problem?} \rightarrow \text{Define defect } D \\
W_2 &: \text{Why is it occurring?} \rightarrow \text{Root cause via 5 Whys} \\
W_3 &: \text{Where is it happening?} \rightarrow \text{Identify process, location, machine} \\
W_4 &: \text{When does it occur?} \rightarrow \text{Time, shift, frequency mapping} \\
W_5 &: \text{Who is involved?} \rightarrow \text{Operator, supervisor, supplier} \\
H_1 &: \text{How will we intervene?} \rightarrow \text{Method, tool, procedure} \\
H_2 &: \text{How much will it cost?} \rightarrow \text{Capital, training, downtime}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This decomposition is isomorphic to a *requirements specification* in software engineering. Each $W_i$ and $H_j$ becomes a *testable acceptance criterion* for the subsequent DO phase.

## 3.2 Hypothesis Construction in PLAN

A PDSA-acceptable change must be a *falsifiable hypothesis* of the form:

$$
H_0 : \text{Intervention } I \text{ will not change } \text{KPI} \\
H_1 : \text{Intervention } I \text{ will change } \text{KPI by at least } \delta
$$

The measurable objective is therefore:

$$
\Delta \text{KPI} = \text{KPI}_{\text{after}} - \text{KPI}_{\text{before}} \geq \delta
$$

where $\delta$ is the **Minimum Practically Significant Difference (MPSD)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Heuristic:** A well-formed PDSA plan must specify a *numeric* $\delta$. Vague objectives like "improve quality" are penalized.

## 3.3 The DO Phase — Data Collection Protocol (Exhaustive)

A KTU full-mark answer for the DO phase must explicitly enumerate the following six activities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DO} \rightarrow \{ &\text{(1) Document baseline}, \\
&\text{(2) Train data collectors}, \\
&\text{(3) Execute pilot on } n \geq 30 \text{ samples}, \\
&\text{(4) Record } X_{\text{before}}, X_{\text{after}} \text{ for each KPI}, \\
&\text{(5) Capture process anomalies}, \\
&\text{(6) Store raw data in a control log} \,\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $n \geq 30$?** The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) guarantees approximate normality of the sample mean, which is the prerequisite for using the $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and $\bar{X} \pm 3\sigma$ control limits in the STUDY phase.

## 3.4 The STUDY Phase — Statistical Decision Algorithm

The STUDY phase applies a **sequential decision algorithm** that converts raw observations into a verdict. The full logical flow is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Compute } \bar{X}_{\text{after}}, s_{\text{after}} \text{ (sample mean and std. dev.)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Compute } C_{pk}^{\text{after}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\bar{X}_{\text{after}} - \text{LSL}}{3 s_{\text{after}}}, \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}_{\text{after}}}{3 s_{\text{after}}}\right) \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Compute } C_{pk}^{\text{before}} \text{ from baseline data} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Compute } \Delta C_{pk} = C_{pk}^{\text{after}} - C_{pk}^{\text{before}} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{If } \Delta C_{pk} \geq 0.33 \text{ AND } C_{pk}^{\text{after}} \geq 1.00 \quad \Rightarrow \text{ADOPT} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{If } 0 < \Delta C_{pk} < 0.33 \quad \Rightarrow \text{ADAPT (new PDSA)} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{If } \Delta C_{pk} \leq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{ABANDON (redefine problem)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Threshold Rationale:** The threshold of $\Delta C_{pk} = 0.33$ corresponds to the industry-standard *minimum practically significant improvement* (one-third of a sigma level), aligning with Six Sigma DMAIC practice.

## 3.5 The ACT Phase — Standardization Mechanics

When the verdict is **ADOPT**, the organization must perform the following *lock-in* actions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ACT-ADOPT} \rightarrow \{ &\text{(1) Update Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)}, \\
&\text{(2) Train all stakeholders (not just pilot team)}, \\
&\text{(3) Update FMEA and control plan}, \\
&\text{(4) Add new control chart to SPC system}, \\
&\text{(5) Begin monitoring the new baseline} \,\}
\end{aligned}
$$

When the verdict is **ADAPT**, the cycle restarts with modified $H_1$. The recursion can be modelled as:

$$
\text{PDSA}_{k+1} = f(\text{PDSA}_k, \text{Lessons Learned}_k)
$$

where $f(\cdot)$ is the *knowledge-integration function* that uses the previous cycle's STUDY output to refine the next PLAN.

## 3.6 Quantitative Worked-Example Framework (Numerical Demo)

Consider a KTU-style numerical question: *"A manufacturing line produces shafts with diameter specification $20.00 \pm 0.10$ mm. The pre-PDSA sample of $n = 30$ gave $\bar{X}_1 = 20.04$ mm, $s_1 = 0.025$ mm. After the DO phase, $n = 30$ gave $\bar{X}_2 = 20.01$ mm, $s_2 = 0.020$ mm. Evaluate the PDSA STUDY verdict."*

**Step 1 — Identify limits:** USL $= 20.10$ mm, LSL $= 19.90$ mm.

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$ before:**

$$
C_{pk}^{\text{before}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{20.04 - 19.90}{3(0.025)}, \dfrac{20.10 - 20.04}{3(0.025)}\right) = \min(1.867, 0.800) = 0.800
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{pk}$ after:**

$$
C_{pk}^{\text{after}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{20.01 - 19.90}{3(0.020)}, \dfrac{20.10 - 20.01}{3(0.020)}\right) = \min(1.833, 1.500) = 1.500
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Delta C_{pk}$:**

$$
\Delta C_{pk} = 1.500 - 0.800 = 0.700
$$

**Step 5 — Apply decision rule:** $\Delta C_{pk} = 0.700 \geq 0.33$ **and** $C_{pk}^{\text{after}} = 1.500 \geq 1.00$. **Verdict: ADOPT.**

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** The process *also* moved closer to the target (centring improved from $20.04$ to $20.01$), which is a *bonus* benefit of the intervention.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Primary Mermaid Diagram — The PDSA Cycle Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Organizational Problem Identified]) --> planA[PLAN Phase]
    planA --> planB[Define Objective SMART]
    planB --> planC[5W2H Analysis]
    planC --> planD[Formulate Hypothesis H1]
    planD --> planE[Select KPIs and MPSD]
    planE --> doA[DO Phase]
    doA --> doB[Train Pilot Team]
    doB --> doC[Collect Baseline Data]
    doC --> doD[Execute Pilot on n greater than 30]
    doD --> doE[Log Anomalies]
    doE --> studyA[STUDY Phase]
    studyA --> studyB[Compute Cpk Before and After]
    studyB --> studyC[Apply Control Charts]
    studyC --> studyD[Compare delta KPI to MPSD]
    studyD --> studyE{Decision Rule}
    studyE -->|Adopt| actA[ACT Adopt]
    studyE -->|Adapt| actB[ACT Adapt Restart PDSA]
    studyE -->|Abandon| actC[ACT Abandon Redefine]
    actA --> std1[Update SOP and FMEA]
    std1 --> std2[Train All Stakeholders]
    std2 --> std3[Rollout to Full Scale]
    std3 --> nextCycle1[Begin New PDSA Cycle]
    actB --> planA
    actC --> planA
    nextCycle1 --> planA
```

## 4.2 Secondary Mermaid Diagram — Tool–Phase Mapping Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLAN_TOOLS
        tool1[Pareto Chart]
        tool2[Fishbone Diagram]
        tool3[5 Whys]
        tool4[5W2H Grid]
        tool5[Affinity Diagram]
    end
    subgraph DO_TOOLS
        tool6[Check Sheets]
        tool7[Gantt Chart]
        tool8[Pilot Run Log]
        tool9[Histogram Baseline]
    end
    subgraph STUDY_TOOLS
        tool10[Control Chart Xbar R]
        tool11[Scatter Diagram]
        tool12[Capability Analysis]
        tool13[Hypothesis Test t Test]
    end
    subgraph ACT_TOOLS
        tool14[Standardization Matrix]
        tool15[FMEA Update]
        tool16[SOP Revision]
        tool17[Training Plan]
    end
    PLAN_TOOLS --> DO_TOOLS
    DO_TOOLS --> STUDY_TOOLS
    STUDY_TOOLS --> ACT_TOOLS
    ACT_TOOLS --> PLAN_TOOLS
```

## 4.3 Tertiary Mermaid Diagram — Decision Logic of the ACT Phase

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input[STUDY Output delta Cpk and Cpk after] --> q1{Is delta Cpk greater than 0.33}
    q1 -->|No| q2{Is delta Cpk greater than 0}
    q1 -->|Yes| q3{Is Cpk after at least 1.00}
    q2 -->|No| abandon[ABANDON Restart with New Problem Statement]
    q2 -->|Yes| adapt[ADAPT Modify Plan and Start New PDSA]
    q3 -->|No| adapt
    q3 -->|Yes| adopt[ADOPT Standardize and Scale Up]
    adopt --> monitor[Add to SPC Monitoring System]
    monitor --> feedback[Initiate Next Improvement Cycle]
    adapt --> feedback
    abandon --> feedback
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

*"Differentiate between the PDSA cycle and PDCA cycle. Which one is preferred in the modern Deming philosophy and why?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle was the original formulation popularised in earlier quality literature, where the third phase "Check" implies a superficial verification against a standard. The **PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act)** cycle, preferred by W. Edwards Deming in his later writings, replaces "Check" with "Study" to emphasise a **deep, analytical, and theory-building** phase. The PDSA variant is preferred in the modern Deming philosophy because it captures the *System of Profound Knowledge* requirement of building a predictive theory of the process, not merely confirming a number. The KTU 2024 TQM syllabus adopts the **PDSA** terminology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Difference (1), Deming's reasoning (1), KTU preference (1).

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

*"List the four phases of the PDSA cycle and state the primary objective of the STUDY phase."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four phases of the PDSA cycle are **Plan, Do, Study, and Act**. The primary objective of the **STUDY** phase is to **analyse the data collected during the DO phase, compare the observed outcomes against the predicted KPIs, and determine whether the change hypothesis is validated, partially validated, or rejected.** This phase uses statistical tools like control charts, Pareto analysis, and process capability indices ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$) to draw evidence-based conclusions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Four phases (1), STUDY objective (2).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Section B questions are offered in *OR* (internal choice) format. The student must answer *either* Question B1 *or* Question B2. Each question carries 7 marks per sub-part.

---

### **Question B1** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain in detail the four phases of the **PDSA cycle** with a suitable real-world example. Discuss the role of **statistical process control** tools in each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *"Continuous improvement is impossible without an iterative learning mechanism."* Critically evaluate this statement in the context of the **PDSA cycle** and the **Deming philosophy of management**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer B1(a) — 7 marks

The PDSA cycle consists of four iterative phases:

**1. PLAN Phase (2 marks):**
In this phase, the team identifies the problem, sets a SMART objective, predicts outcomes, and designs a data-collection plan. A real-world example is a hospital aiming to reduce patient-waiting time from 45 minutes to under 20 minutes in the OPD. Tools used here include the 5W2H grid, Pareto chart, fishbone diagram, and 5-Whys analysis.

**2. DO Phase (1.5 marks):**
The plan is executed on a small pilot scale (e.g., one OPD wing for two weeks). The team collects baseline data and pilot data using check sheets and run charts. Anomalies and unexpected observations are logged.

**3. STUDY Phase (2 marks):**
The data is analysed. The waiting-time mean drops from 45 to 22 minutes, and the standard deviation reduces. A control chart shows special-cause variation eliminated. The hypothesis is *partially* validated — improvement is real but the target (20 min) is not fully met. A new PDSA cycle is required.

**4. ACT Phase (1.5 marks):**
Because the result is *partially* successful, the team chooses to **ADAPT** — it adopts the new triage process, modifies the appointment slot duration, and re-enters PLAN with a refined hypothesis. The standardisation is partial, and the cycle restarts.

**Role of SPC Tools:** In PLAN, SPC helps define realistic process capability targets. In DO, run charts and check sheets capture data. In STUDY, control charts (X-bar, R, p-charts) and $C_{pk}$ analysis distinguish common-cause and special-cause variation. In ACT, the validated control limits become the new operational baseline for the SPC system.

---

#### Model Answer B1(b) — 7 marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The statement is a foundational principle of the Deming philosophy. Continuous improvement (Kaizen) requires *learning*, and learning requires *iteration*. The PDSA cycle is the structural embodiment of this principle.

**Argument in Favour (3 marks):**
1. **Knowledge Building:** Every PDSA rotation converts tacit assumptions into explicit, testable knowledge. Without iteration, organizations operate on hearsay.
2. **Reduction of Resistance to Change:** Small iterative cycles lower psychological resistance compared to big-bang transformations.
3. **Adaptation to Variation:** Real systems exhibit common-cause and special-cause variation. Only iterative testing reveals which variation is present.
4. **Statistical Rigor:** A single change cycle provides one data point; multiple cycles allow trend analysis and process-capability tracking.

**Counter-Argument & Nuance (1.5 marks):**
1. PDSA assumes management's commitment to a *learning organisation*; without it, the cycle becomes a bureaucratic ritual.
2. PDSA may be slow for crisis situations requiring rapid deployment (e.g., pandemic response).

**Conclusion (1.5 marks):**
The statement is **largely true**. PDSA is not merely a tool but a *management philosophy* aligned with the Deming System of Profound Knowledge. Organizations that institutionalise PDSA outperform those that do not in long-term quality outcomes.

---

### **Question B2** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, 2024 Scheme Pattern]

**(a)** Discuss the **origin and evolution** of the PDSA cycle. Explain how the cycle differs from traditional project-management approaches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A precision-machining workshop produces bearings with diameter specification **$25.00 \pm 0.05$ mm**. The team runs a PDSA cycle. **Pre-intervention data (n = 30):** $\bar{X}_1 = 25.03$ mm, $s_1 = 0.012$ mm. **Post-intervention data (n = 30):** $\bar{X}_2 = 25.005$ mm, $s_2 = 0.008$ mm. Compute the **process capability index** before and after, and recommend the appropriate ACT-phase decision. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer B2(a) — 7 marks

**Origin and Evolution (3.5 marks):**
The PDSA cycle originated in the 1920s at **Bell Laboratories** through the work of **Walter A. Shewhart**, the father of statistical quality control. Shewhart conceptualised it as a scientific method of iterative learning for industrial processes. In the 1950s, **W. Edwards Deming** popularised and modified the cycle during his lectures in post-WWII Japan, where it became the backbone of Japanese industrial revival. Over the decades, the cycle evolved from a manufacturing tool to a universal improvement framework, adopted by healthcare, education, software (Agile), and service industries. The modern version emphasises *systems thinking* and *psychology* alongside statistical analysis.

**Difference from Traditional Project Management (3.5 marks):**

| Dimension | PDSA Cycle | Traditional Project Mgmt |
|---|---|---|
| Scale | Small, iterative pilots | Big-bang, one-time rollout |
| Learning | Built into every rotation | Limited to post-mortem |
| Risk | Low (small pilot) | High (full-scale) |
| Feedback | Continuous (per cycle) | Delayed (end of project) |
| Tools | SPC, 5 Whys, Fishbone | Gantt, PERT, CPM |
| Philosophy | Learning organisation | Deliverable-focused |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Cue:** When the question says *"critically evaluate"*, the answer *must* contain both an argument and a counter-argument or limitation. A one-sided answer caps at ~5/7 marks.

---

#### Model Answer B2(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify Specification Limits (1 mark):**
USL $= 25.05$ mm, LSL $= 24.95$ mm.

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$ Before Intervention (2 marks):**

$$
C_{pk}^{\text{before}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{25.03 - 24.95}{3(0.012)}, \dfrac{25.05 - 25.03}{3(0.012)}\right)
$$

$$
C_{pk}^{\text{before}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{0.08}{0.036}, \dfrac{0.02}{0.036}\right) = \min(2.222, 0.556) = 0.556
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{pk}$ After Intervention (2 marks):**

$$
C_{pk}^{\text{after}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{25.005 - 24.95}{3(0.008)}, \dfrac{25.05 - 25.005}{3(0.008)}\right)
$$

$$
C_{pk}^{\text{after}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{0.055}{0.024}, \dfrac{0.045}{0.024}\right) = \min(2.292, 1.875) = 1.875
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Delta C_{pk}$ and Decide (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta C_{pk} = 1.875 - 0.556 = 1.319
$$

Since $\Delta C_{pk} = 1.319 \geq 0.33$ **and** $C_{pk}^{\text{after}} = 1.875 \geq 1.00$, the **ACT-phase verdict is ADOPT**. The team should standardise the intervention by updating the SOP, training all operators, and rolling out the change to all production lines. The new baseline becomes the input to the *next* PDSA cycle aimed at pushing $C_{pk}$ toward $2.00$ (Six-Sigma territory).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. Computing $C_p$ instead of $C_{pk}$ → loses 1 mark. Always use $C_{pk}$ when the process mean is *not* centred at the target.
> 2. Forgetting the $\min(\cdot)$ function → loses 1 mark.
> 3. Failing to state the ACT decision explicitly → loses 1 mark.
> 4. Not commenting on the centring effect (here, $\bar{X}$ moved from $25.03$ to $25.005$, almost perfectly centred) → loses 0.5 mark for incomplete analysis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- **PDSA = Plan, Do, Study, Act** — the *only* accepted acronym in KTU 2024 scheme.
- Origin: **Shewhart (1920s)** → popularised by **Deming (1950s Japan)**.
- PDSA is the **operational engine of continuous improvement (Kaizen)** and the **Deming philosophy**.
- The cycle is **iterative and recursive** — never terminate after the ACT phase.
- **PLAN** = 5W2H, SMART objective, falsifiable hypothesis with MPSD.
- **DO** = small-scale pilot, baseline data, $n \geq 30$ samples preferred.
- **STUDY** = control charts, $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, hypothesis testing, comparison against MPSD.
- **ACT** = three decisions: **Adopt, Adapt, Abandon** — each triggers a different follow-up.
- Decision rule: $\Delta C_{pk} \geq 0.33$ **and** $C_{pk}^{\text{after}} \geq 1.00$ $\Rightarrow$ **Adopt**.
- Always use $C_{pk}$ (not $C_p$) when the process is **not centred** at the specification midpoint.
- PDSA differs from PDCA: **Study** $>$ **Check** (Study implies deeper analysis and theory-building).
- PDSA differs from traditional project management: **iterative pilots** vs **big-bang rollout**.
- Common tools by phase: PLAN → Pareto, Fishbone, 5W2H; DO → Check sheets, Gantt; STUDY → Control charts, $C_{pk}$; ACT → SOPs, FMEA, training plans.
- The cycle aligns with the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**: PLAN = Apply/Analyze, DO = Apply, STUDY = Evaluate, ACT = Create.
- **Real-world applications** include manufacturing, healthcare, software (Agile), education (NEP 2020), and civil engineering.
- **Common student error:** Terminating the cycle after ACT — examiners explicitly test for recursion awareness.
- **Common student error:** Confusing $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ — always pick $C_{pk}$ for off-centre processes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kaizen and Supplier Partnership: Partnering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT636 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Kaizen and Supplier Partnership: Partnering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Kaizen?

**Kaizen** is a Japanese management philosophy originating from the Toyota Production System, which translates to *"Continuous Improvement"* (改 善 — *Kai* = Change, *Zen* = Good/Better). It is the practice of identifying, implementing, and sustaining small, incremental improvements in processes, products, or services on a continuous basis with the active involvement of **every employee** — from the CEO to the shop-floor worker.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Total Quality Management Context):**
> *Kaizen is a Japanese term for "continuous improvement" — a long-term, systematic approach to gradually improve processes, products, and services through small, incremental changes driven by employee involvement at all levels of the organization.*

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Daily Five-Minute Clean-up"**
>
> Imagine you share a kitchen with three flatmates. Every day, instead of waiting for the kitchen to become a complete disaster and then spending a full Saturday cleaning it (the "Big Bang" approach), each flatmate spends just **5 minutes every evening** wiping the counter, organizing the spice rack, and throwing out expired food. Over weeks and months, the kitchen remains perpetually clean, and no single person is ever overwhelmed.
>
> **That "5 minutes daily" habit is Kaizen.** It avoids dramatic, expensive overhauls by stacking tiny wins consistently. In a factory, this might mean a worker noticing that a bolt is positioned awkwardly and suggesting moving it 2 cm closer — saving 3 seconds per cycle. Over 1 million cycles per year, that is **833 hours saved**. That is the power of Kaizen.

---

## 1.2 What is Supplier Partnership?

A **Supplier Partnership** (also called *Strategic Supplier Alliance*) is a long-term, cooperative, win-win relationship between a buyer (customer organization) and its key suppliers, founded on **mutual trust, shared vision, open communication, and joint problem-solving**, rather than the traditional adversarial price-driven "arm's-length" transaction model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Supplier Partnership is a long-term, cooperative relationship between a buyer and a supplier based on mutual trust, openness, shared risk, and shared rewards, designed to leverage the combined capabilities of both organizations to create competitive advantage for the entire supply chain.*

---

## 1.3 What is "Partnering"?

**Partnering** is the *philosophical and operational framework* that transforms a traditional buyer-supplier relationship into a **strategic partnership**. It moves away from multi-supplier, low-bidder, contract-driven purchasing to a **single-source, long-term, integrated relationship** where both parties co-invest in improvement.

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy — "The Marriage vs. The One-Night Stand"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:**
>
> A traditional supplier relationship is like **dating** — you evaluate multiple partners, you keep your wallet close, you switch the moment someone offers a slightly better deal, and there is no emotional investment.
>
> A supplier **partnership** is like a **marriage** — you commit long-term, you share finances, you raise children (products) together, and when one partner faces a crisis (raw material price hike), the other stands by them rather than divorcing.
>
> Partnering is the **wedding ceremony and the vows** that formalize this marriage — the structured process of trust-building, goal-alignment, and reciprocal investment.

---

## 1.4 The Triad: Kaizen + Supplier Partnership + Partnering

These three concepts are **deeply interconnected** in TQM:

| Concept | Role in TQM | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Kaizen** | Engine of continuous improvement | Better processes daily |
| **Supplier Partnership** | Vehicle for extending improvement across the supply chain | Joint value creation |
| **Partnering** | Framework / Strategy | Long-term cooperative model |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU expects students to understand that *Partnering is the strategy, Supplier Partnership is the structure, and Kaizen is the continuous-improvement philosophy that fuels the partnership once it is established.*

---

## 1.5 Core Pillars of Kaizen (Foundation Vocabulary)

Before diving deep, here are **5 foundational Kaizen pillars** every KTU student must memorize:

1. **Gemba** — "The actual place" where value is created (the shop floor, the call center, the kitchen).
2. **PDCA / Deming Cycle** — **P**lan → **D**o → **C**heck → **A**ct.
3. **5 Whys** — Ask "why" five times to drill to the root cause of a problem.
4. **5S** — **S**ort, **S**et in Order, **S**hine, **S**tandardize, **S**ustain.
5. **Genchi Genbutsu** — "Go and see for yourself" (empirical observation, not reports).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 The Anatomy of Kaizen

Kaizen is built on **three operational layers**:

### Layer 1 — Philosophy
- Improvement is **never-ending**; no process is ever "perfect."
- Improvements must be **incremental and low-cost** (not capital-intensive).
- **Everyone** participates — from the Managing Director to the housekeeping staff.

### Layer 2 — Tools (The Kaizen Toolbox)

| Tool | Purpose | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **5S** | Workplace organization | Foundation for any improvement |
| **PDCA** | Structured experimentation | When testing any change |
| **5 Whys** | Root-cause analysis | When a problem occurs |
| **Pareto Chart (80/20)** | Priority setting | When many issues exist |
| **Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone / Ishikawa)** | Identifying causes | Complex problems |
| **Kanban** | Visual workflow control | Inventory/production flow |
| **Poka-Yoke** | Mistake-proofing | To eliminate human error |
| **Kaizen Blitz / Event** | Rapid 3-5 day improvement sprint | Focused, scoped problems |

### Layer 3 — Cultural Mindset
- *Suggestion systems*, *quality circles*, *gemba walks* by managers, *just-in-time* thinking.

---

## 2.2 Why Kaizen Matters — The Mathematical Logic

Kaizen operates on a deceptively simple exponential-compounding principle. A small daily improvement compounds into massive annual gain.

Let:
- $i$ = daily improvement fraction (e.g., $0.001$ = **0.1 % per day**)
- $n$ = number of working days per year ≈ **250**
- $P_0$ = baseline performance metric

Then after one year:

$$P_{1} = P_{0} \cdot (1 + i)^{n}$$

For $i = 0.001$ and $n = 250$:

$$P_{1} = P_{0} \cdot (1.001)^{250} \approx P_{0} \cdot 1.284$$

That is, a **0.1 % daily improvement** yields a **~28.4 % annual improvement** — *without any massive investment*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the same mathematical structure as **compound interest**. Kaizen is the *interest rate* on operational excellence.

---

## 2.3 Anatomy of Supplier Partnership

A supplier partnership has **six defining structural elements**:

1. **Long-term horizon** (typically 3–10 years, not transactional single orders).
2. **Single-sourcing or limited-sourcing** (not chasing the lowest bidder).
3. **Mutual trust and transparency** (open-book cost-sharing).
4. **Joint problem-solving and innovation**.
5. **Shared risk and shared reward**.
6. **Integration of systems** (EDI, shared ERP, joint quality audits).

---

## 2.4 Partnering — The Strategic Framework

Partnering is the **process** of establishing and sustaining a supplier partnership. The framework is summarized as the **"Partnering Charter"** consisting of:

### 🔸 The Four Pillars of Partnering (Trademark of the Construction Industry Institute, USA — widely cited in TQM literature)

| Pillar | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Mutual Trust** | Willingness to share vulnerabilities and information |
| **Mutual Goals** | Common objectives beyond individual contracts |
| **Mutual Commitment** | Top-management buy-in to long-term success |
| **Mutual Performance Measurement** | Joint KPIs reviewed regularly |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All mathematical vertical bars use `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept / Formula | Equation or Definition | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kaizen Compound Improvement Factor | $F = (1 + i)^{n}$ | Dimensionless multiplier |
| 2 | Annual Improvement % | $\Delta P = \left[(1+i)^{n} - 1\right] \cdot 100$ | Percent (\%) |
| 3 | Supplier Partnership Duration | $T_{\text{partnership}} \geq 3$ years | Years |
| 4 | Weighted Partner-Selection Score | $S_{j} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} w_{k} \cdot r_{jk}$ | Score, $0 \leq S_j \leq 5$ |
| 5 | Supplier Defect Rate (PPM) | $\text{PPM} = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units supplied}} \cdot 10^{6}$ | Parts per million |
| 6 | On-Time Delivery (OTD) % | $\text{OTD} = \dfrac{\text{Deliveries on time}}{\text{Total deliveries}} \cdot 100$ | Percent (\%) |
| 7 | 5 Whys Depth | Root cause found by $\leq 5$ iterations | Count |
| 8 | PDCA Cycle Time | One full rotation per improvement cycle | Days / Weeks |

Where:
- $i$ = incremental daily improvement fraction (dimensionless)
- $n$ = number of working days
- $w_k$ = weight of criterion $k$ (with $\sum w_k = 1$)
- $r_{jk}$ = rating of supplier $j$ on criterion $k$ (typically 1–5 scale)

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (Toyota, Tata Motors)**: Kaizen events reduce cycle time by 20–40 % in a week.
- **Aerospace (Boeing, HAL)**: Long-term partnerships with single-tier-1 suppliers (e.g., GE, Rolls-Royce) enable co-engineering.
- **Construction (L&T, Afcons)**: Partnering charters reduced project disputes by ~70 %.
- **IT Services (Infosys, TCS)**: Kaizen applied to ticket resolution and code review.
- **Healthcare (Apollo, AIIMS)**: 5S in operation theatres reduced surgical-site infections.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Kaizen Compounding Power

**Problem:** A factory produces 10,000 units/day. A worker proposes a Kaizen that reduces cycle time by 0.05 % per day. Assuming **250 working days/year**, what is the cumulative annual production gain?

**Given:**
- $P_0 = 10{,}000$ units/day
- $i = 0.0005$ (i.e., 0.05 % per day)
- $n = 250$ days

**Step 1 — Apply the Kaizen compounding formula.**

$$\text{Factor } F = (1 + i)^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$F = (1 + 0.0005)^{250}$$

**Step 3 — Compute using binomial approximation $\ln(1+x) \approx x$ for small $x$.**

$$\ln(F) = 250 \cdot \ln(1.0005) \approx 250 \cdot 0.0005 = 0.125$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate to recover $F$.**

$$F = e^{0.125} \approx 1.1331$$

**Step 5 — Compute the new daily capacity.**

$$P_{1} = P_{0} \cdot F = 10{,}000 \cdot 1.1331 = 11{,}331 \text{ units/day}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the annual gain.**

$$\Delta P_{\text{annual}} = P_{1} - P_{0} = 1{,}331 \text{ units/day}$$

$$\Delta P_{\text{year}} = 1{,}331 \cdot 250 = 332{,}750 \text{ additional units/year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A seemingly trivial 0.05 % daily improvement yields **~3.33 lakh extra units per year** without any capital expenditure. This is the mathematical proof of Kaizen's power.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Weighted Partner Selection Matrix (Analytical Decision Tool)

**Scenario:** A Kerala-based electronics manufacturer must select one long-term **partner-supplier** for a critical printed-circuit-board (PCB) component. Five candidates (S1 to S5) have been rated on four criteria. Compute the best partner using the weighted-score method.

| Criterion | Weight ($w_k$) | S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | S5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality (PPM) | 0.35 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 3 |
| On-Time Delivery | 0.25 | 5 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 4 |
| Cost-Competitiveness | 0.20 | 3 | 5 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| Technical Capability | 0.20 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 5 | 3 |

(Ratings on a 1–5 scale, where 5 = best.)

**Step 1 — Verify weights sum to unity.**

$$\sum_{k=1}^{4} w_k = 0.35 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 1.00 \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute weighted score for supplier S1.**

$$S_{1} = (0.35)(4) + (0.25)(5) + (0.20)(3) + (0.20)(4)$$

$$S_{1} = 1.40 + 1.25 + 0.60 + 0.80 = 4.05$$

**Step 3 — Compute score for supplier S2.**

$$S_{2} = (0.35)(3) + (0.25)(4) + (0.20)(5) + (0.20)(3)$$

$$S_{2} = 1.05 + 1.00 + 1.00 + 0.60 = 3.65$$

**Step 4 — Compute score for supplier S3.**

$$S_{3} = (0.35)(5) + (0.25)(4) + (0.20)(3) + (0.20)(5)$$

$$S_{3} = 1.75 + 1.00 + 0.60 + 1.00 = 4.35$$

**Step 5 — Compute score for supplier S4.**

$$S_{4} = (0.35)(4) + (0.25)(3) + (0.20)(4) + (0.20)(5)$$

$$S_{4} = 1.40 + 0.75 + 0.80 + 1.00 = 3.95$$

**Step 6 — Compute score for supplier S5.**

$$S_{5} = (0.35)(3) + (0.25)(4) + (0.20)(5) + (0.20)(3)$$

$$S_{5} = 1.05 + 1.00 + 1.00 + 0.60 = 3.65$$

**Step 7 — Rank the suppliers.**

| Rank | Supplier | Score |
|---|---|---|
| 🥇 1st | **S3** | **4.35** |
| 2nd | S1 | 4.05 |
| 3rd | S4 | 3.95 |
| 4th (tie) | S2 | 3.65 |
| 4th (tie) | S5 | 3.65 |

**Step 8 — Conclusion.**

$$\boxed{\text{Supplier S3 is selected as the long-term partner.}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** This weighted-score method is the *analytical engine* of partnering — it transforms an emotional "I like vendor X" decision into an objective, defensible, auditable choice. Examiners love this method because it allows both numerical reasoning and management interpretation.

---

## 3.3 The Step-by-Step Partnering Process (Analytical Roadmap)

The full partnering life-cycle, expanded into auditable phases:

### Phase 1 — Strategic Identification
Identify which commodities/services warrant a partnership (typically the top 20 % by spend — **Pareto's 80/20**).

### Phase 2 — Partner Selection
Use the **Weighted Partner Selection Matrix** (Section 3.2).

### Phase 3 — Partnering Charter
Formally draft a **Partnering Charter** — a one-page document signed by both CEOs stating:
- Vision, mission, mutual goals, KPIs, escalation path.

### Phase 4 — Joint Performance Measurement
Set up **Mutual KPIs**: OTD %, PPM defects, cost-reduction, innovation index.

### Phase 5 — Continuous Improvement (Kaizen inside the Partnership)
The supplier is treated as an **extended enterprise**. Joint Kaizen teams visit Gemba together.

### Phase 6 — Review and Renewal
Annual **Partnering Review Meeting** — celebrate wins, identify gaps, renew contract.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Kaizen Engine (PDCA + 5S + Kaizen Event Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify Problem at Gemba"]:::start --> B["Apply 5S Workplace Foundation"]
    B --> C["Plan Improvement - PDCA Phase 1"]
    C --> D["Do Small Experiment - PDCA Phase 2"]
    D --> E["Check Results - PDCA Phase 3"]
    E --> F{Qas Improvement Achieved?}
    F -- Yes --> G["Act - Standardize New Method"]
    F -- No --> A
    G --> H["Document in Kaizen Suggestion System"]
    H --> I["Train All Employees"]
    I --> J["Next Gemba Problem Surfaced"]
    J --> A

    classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — The Supplier Partnership Tier Architecture

```mermaid

    end

    end

    T1 -->|Kaizen spillover| T2
```
