In the West, QFD was popularized in the mid-1980s through the work of **John Hauser and Don Clausing (1988, HBR)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Philosophy:** QFD transforms *subjective customer feelings* into *objective engineering specifications*. It is essentially a four-phase planning tool used in concurrent engineering and TQM.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to throw a **dream birthday party** for a 6-year-old child:

- **Customer (the child)** says: *"I want fun, lots of cake, my friends, magic tricks, and a return gift."* — These are fuzzy, emotional **Whats** (Voice of Customer).
- You, as the planner, must translate them into **Hows** (actionable specifics):
  - *Fun* → Games, music, 90-minute duration
  - *Cake* → 2 kg chocolate, eggless, 8-inch diameter
  - *Friends* → 12 children, mixed-age group
  - *Magic tricks* → Hire a magician, 30-minute show
  - *Return gift* → Budget ₹200 per child, toys + chocolates

**QFD does exactly this — but for products and services.** It is the *engineering translator* between **"What the customer wants"** and **"How the engineering team will build it."** Without QFD, the bakery may make a wedding cake instead of a kid's cake!

> [!TIP]
> **In one line:** *QFD = Listening to the customer + Building a precise engineering blueprint simultaneously.*

---

### 1.3 Standard QFD Metrics (KTU High-Yield Terms)

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **VOC** | Voice of Customer | Raw inputs from customers |
| **Whats** | Customer Requirements (CRs) | Rows of HOQ |
| **Hows** | Technical Requirements (TRs) | Columns of HOQ |
| **Roof** | Correlation Matrix | Hows interact with Hows |
| **Relationship Matrix** | Whats vs Hows | Central body of HOQ |
| **SI** | Sales/Strategic Importance | Weighted priority of CRs |
| **WI** | Weight/Importance | Normalized priority for TRs |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse:** QFD is *not* just one matrix. It is a **4-phase deployment system** — only **Phase 1** uses the famous House of Quality.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Phases of QFD Deployment

QFD is deployed in **four sequential phases**, each producing a matrix that feeds the next:

1. **Phase I – Product Planning (House of Quality):** Translates VOC into design requirements.
2. **Phase II – Product Design (Deployment Matrix):** Deploys the *Hows* of Phase I into *subsystem/part characteristics*.
3. **Phase III – Process Planning:** Converts part characteristics into *process parameters* and *manufacturing controls*.
4. **Phase IV – Production Planning:** Defines *production controls*, *operator instructions*, and *quality checkpoints*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Explain the four phases of QFD."* — Always answer sequentially with the input/output of each phase.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Construction of the House of Quality (HOQ)

The House of Quality is built in **six logical steps**:

1. **List Customer Requirements (Whats).** Capture VOC using tools like focus groups, surveys, KJ analysis, Kano model. Usually 20–30 items.
2. **Assign Importance Ratings (IR).** Customers rate each requirement on a **1–5 scale** (1 = not important, 5 = very important).
3. **List Technical Requirements (Hows).** Engineering team translates CRs into measurable engineering metrics (e.g., *torque → Nm*, *fuel efficiency → km/L*).
4. **Build the Relationship Matrix (centre).** Symbols used:
   - **⊙ (Strong)** = 9 points
   - **○ (Medium)** = 3 points
   - **△ (Weak)** = 1 point
5. **Build the Roof (Correlation Matrix).** Shows how TRs interact with each other:
   - **⊕ (Strong Positive)** = promote each other
   - **⊖ (Strong Negative)** = conflict
   - **○ (Weak Positive)**, **▬ (Weak Negative)**
6. **Calculate Weighted Importance of Technical Requirements** (the right-side column).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations govern QFD prioritization. Every symbol uses standard scoring conventions and **no vertical pipes** are placed inside table cells.

$$
\text{Raw Importance of CR}_i \;=\; \text{Survey score of requirement } i
$$

$$
\text{Weighted Score of CR}_i \;=\; \text{Importance}_i \times \text{Sales Point}_i
$$

$$
\text{Normalized Weight}_i \;=\; \frac{\text{Weighted Score}_i}{\sum_{k=1}^{n}\text{Weighted Score}_k} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Weighted Importance of TR}_j \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\text{Importance of CR}_i \times \text{Relationship}_{ij}\right)
$$

| Parameter | Symbol | Range / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Importance Rating (Customer) | $IR_i$ | 1 to 5 (dimensionless) | Prioritises each CR |
| Sales Point | $SP_i$ | 1.0 / 1.2 / 1.5 (dimensionless) | Strategic uplift |
| Relationship Score | $R_{ij}$ | 1 / 3 / 9 (points) | Whats–Hows link strength |
| Correlation (Roof) | $C_{jk}$ | Symbol based | How–How interaction |
| Technical Weight | $TW_j$ | Sum (points) | Ranks TRs for deployment |
| Absolute Weight | $AW_j$ | Percentage (%) | Normalised priority for engineering |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Scoring Convention (memorize):** Strong = **9**, Medium = **3**, Weak = **1**, No link = **0**.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Translates "smooth ride" into suspension damping coefficient, wheelbase, and tyre pressure specifications.
- **Software:** Translates "user-friendly" into response time, number of clicks, screen transitions.
- **Healthcare:** Translates "patient comfort" into room temperature, noise level (dB), waiting time (min).
- **Aerospace:** Boeing, Airbus, and ISRO use multi-phase QFD for satellite subsystem design.
- **Service Industry:** Used in banking, hospitality, and education for translating customer satisfaction drivers into operational metrics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Insight:** QFD reduces design change costs by **30–50%** because customer needs are captured *before* detailed engineering, not after.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Example: Building a House of Quality for an Electric Kettle

Let us work out a **complete QFD analysis** for an electric kettle — a classic KTU-style numerical problem.

**Customer Requirements (Whats)** — collected from 100 customers:

| # | Customer Requirement | Importance Rating (1–5) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Boils water quickly | 5 |
| 2 | Energy efficient | 4 |
| 3 | Easy to pour | 4 |
| 4 | Cool-touch handle | 5 |
| 5 | Long cord | 3 |
| 6 | Auto shut-off | 5 |
| 7 | Affordable price | 4 |
| 8 | Stylish look | 3 |

**Technical Requirements (Hows)** chosen by engineering:

- **A:** Power rating (W)  
- **B:** Insulation thickness (mm)  
- **C:** Spout angle (°)  
- **D:** Cord length (m)  
- **E:** Thermal sensor accuracy (°C)  
- **F:** Body material finish (index 1–10)  
- **G:** Manufacturing cost (₹)

---

### 3.2 Step 1: Build the Relationship Matrix

Each cell shows the strength of the link between a CR (row) and a TR (column):

| CR \ TR | A | B | C | D | E | F | G |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Boils fast | ⊙ | ○ | △ |  |  |  | △ |
| 2. Energy efficient | ⊙ | △ |  |  | △ |  | ⊖ |
| 3. Easy to pour |  |  | ⊙ |  |  | △ |  |
| 4. Cool handle | △ | ⊙ |  |  |  | △ |  |
| 5. Long cord |  |  |  | ⊙ |  |  | △ |
| 6. Auto shut-off | △ |  |  |  | ⊙ |  |  |
| 7. Affordable |  |  |  | △ |  |  | ⊙ |
| 8. Stylish look |  |  | △ |  |  | ⊙ | ○ |

Scoring: ⊙ = 9, ○ = 3, △ = 1, blank = 0.

---

### 3.3 Step 2: Compute Technical Importance $TW_j$ for each TR

Formula used:
$$
TW_j \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{8} IR_i \cdot R_{ij}
$$

Let us compute one column at a time.

**For TR — A (Power rating, W):**
$$
TW_A = (5)(9) + (4)(9) + (4)(0) + (5)(1) + (3)(0) + (5)(1) + (4)(0) + (3)(0)
$$

$$
TW_A = 45 + 36 + 0 + 5 + 0 + 5 + 0 + 0 = 91
$$

**For TR — B (Insulation thickness, mm):**
$$
TW_B = (5)(3) + (4)(1) + (4)(0) + (5)(9) + (3)(0) + (5)(0) + (4)(0) + (3)(0)
$$

$$
TW_B = 15 + 4 + 0 + 45 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 64
$$

**For TR — C (Spout angle, °):**
$$
TW_C = (5)(1) + (4)(0) + (4)(9) + (5)(0) + (3)(0) + (5)(0) + (4)(0) + (3)(1)
$$

$$
TW_C = 5 + 0 + 36 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 3 = 44
$$

**For TR — D (Cord length, m):**
$$
TW_D = (5)(0) + (4)(0) + (4)(0) + (5)(0) + (3)(9) + (5)(0) + (4)(1) + (3)(0)
$$

$$
TW_D = 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 27 + 0 + 4 + 0 = 31
$$

**For TR — E (Thermal sensor accuracy, °C):**
$$
TW_E = (5)(0) + (4)(1) + (4)(0) + (5)(0) + (3)(0) + (5)(9) + (4)(0) + (3)(0)
$$

$$
TW_E = 0 + 4 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 45 + 0 + 0 = 49
$$

**For TR — F (Body finish, index):**
$$
TW_F = (5)(0) + (4)(0) + (4)(1) + (5)(1) + (3)(0) + (5)(0) + (4)(0) + (3)(9)
$$

$$
TW_F = 0 + 0 + 4 + 5 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 27 = 36
$$

**For TR — G (Manufacturing cost, ₹):**
$$
TW_G = (5)(1) + (4)(-9) + (4)(0) + (5)(0) + (3)(1) + (5)(0) + (4)(9) + (3)(3)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative correlation logic:** $R_{ij} = -9$ for a strong negative link (e.g., higher power (A) raises cost (G); higher finish (F) raises cost (G) — but here the direct link is between "Affordable" and "Cost". Using the matrix entries above, we keep them as positives; negative signs enter via the **roof matrix** when scoring inter-TR conflicts. For this example we use the matrix values as placed (all non-negative symbols).

$$
TW_G = 5 + 4(0) + 0 + 0 + 3 + 0 + 36 + 9 = 53
$$

> Using the **direct matrix scores** (all ⊙=9, ○=3, △=1, blank=0) without injecting roof-correlations into $TW_j$:
>
> $TW_G = 5 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 3 + 0 + 36 + 9 = 53$

---

### 3.4 Step 3: Rank and Normalise

Total Technical Weight:
$$
TW_{\text{total}} = 91 + 64 + 44 + 31 + 49 + 36 + 53 = 368
$$

Normalised importance (Absolute Weight, $AW_j$):
$$
AW_j \;=\; \frac{TW_j}{TW_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

| TR | $TW_j$ | $AW_j$ (%) | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|
| A — Power rating | 91 | 24.73 % | **1** |
| B — Insulation | 64 | 17.39 % | **2** |
| G — Cost control | 53 | 14.40 % | **3** |
| E — Sensor accuracy | 49 | 13.32 % | **4** |
| C — Spout angle | 44 | 11.96 % | **5** |
| F — Body finish | 36 | 9.78 % | **6** |
| D — Cord length | 31 | 8.42 % | **7** |

**Engineering Decision (KTU-style interpretation):**

> [!TIP]
> Since **Power Rating (A)** has the highest absolute weight (≈ 24.7 %), the engineering team should focus first on optimising wattage to satisfy the dominant customer needs (*fast boiling*, *energy efficiency*). **Cord length (D)** is least critical and may be a candidate for cost reduction.

---

### 3.5 Step 4: Add Sales Point (Strategic Importance)

If marketing assigns a **Sales Point** ($SP_i$) to each CR — e.g., *Boils fast* = 1.5, *Auto shut-off* = 1.5, others = 1.0 — the **Strategic Weighted Score** becomes:

$$
SWS_i \;=\; IR_i \times SP_i
$$

These $SWS_i$ values then replace $IR_i$ in the $TW_j$ formula to align engineering priorities with **strategic differentiators**, not just average customer opinions. This is the difference between a *technically excellent* product and a *commercially successful* product.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The House of Quality — Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Customer Requirements Whats"]:::whats
    nodeB["Importance Ratings 1 to 5"]:::rating
    nodeC["Relationship Matrix Centre Symbols"]:::matrix
    nodeD["Technical Requirements Hows Engineering Metrics"]:::hows
    nodeE["Correlation Roof How vs How Interactions"]:::roof
    nodeF["Weighted Importance Absolute Weight Percent"]:::weight
    nodeG["Competitive Benchmark Column"]:::bench
    nodeH["Target Values Engineering Specifications"]:::target

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeD --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeG --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeF

    classDef whats fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000
    classDef rating fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
    classDef matrix fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000
    classDef hows fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef roof fill:#e6b8af,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef weight fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#741b47,color:#000
    classDef bench fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
    classDef target fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** Customer requirements feed importance ratings, which combine with engineering requirements through the relationship matrix. The roof detects conflicting technical features, while the benchmark column compares with competitors. Everything converges into weighted importance and target values that drive engineering specifications.

---

### 4.2 The Four Phases of QFD Deployment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1["Phase 1 Product Planning House of Quality"]:::p1
    p2["Phase 2 Product Design Deployment Matrix"]:::p2
    p3["Phase 3 Process Planning Process Matrix"]:::p3
    p4["Phase 4 Production Planning Control Matrix"]:::p4

    p1 -- Customer Requirements --> p1
    p1 -- Engineering Requirements Output --> p2
    p2 -- Subsystem Characteristics Output --> p3
    p3 -- Process Parameters Output --> p4
    p4 -- Operator and Quality Outputs --> P4END(("Final Product Meets VOC"))

    classDef p1 fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc4125,color:#000
    classDef p2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef p3 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
    classDef p4 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Each phase has its **own matrix** with the same structural logic as the House of Quality, but with progressively more detailed inputs/outputs.

---

### 4.3 Symbol Legend (QFD Visual Convention)

| Symbol | Meaning | Score |
|---|---|---|
| ⊙ | Strong relationship | 9 |
| ○ | Medium relationship | 3 |
| △ | Weak relationship | 1 |
| (blank) | No relationship | 0 |
| ⊕ | Strong positive correlation (roof) | Synergy |
| ⊖ | Strong negative correlation (roof) | Conflict |
| ↑ | Direction of improvement desired | Higher is better |
| ↓ | Direction of improvement desired | Lower is better |
| ◎ | Strong competitive advantage | Benchmark |
| ○ (benchmark) | Parity with competitor | Benchmark |
| △ (benchmark) | Slight disadvantage | Benchmark |

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define Quality Function Deployment (QFD). State any two benefits of implementing QFD in product development.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Quality Function Deployment (QFD) is a structured, customer-driven planning methodology that uses a series of matrices, most notably the **House of Quality (HOQ)**, to translate the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** into measurable **technical/engineering requirements** throughout the product development cycle. It was developed by **Dr. Yoji Akao** in Japan during the 1970s.

**Benefits (1 mark, any two):**
1. Reduces design change costs by capturing customer needs early.
2. Improves cross-functional communication between marketing, design, and manufacturing.
3. Shortens product development time through parallel/concurrent engineering.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *List the components of a House of Quality and briefly explain the "Roof" of the House of Quality.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Components of HOQ (1.5 marks):**
1. Customer Requirements (Whats)
2. Importance Ratings
3. Technical Requirements (Hows)
4. Relationship Matrix (centre)
5. Correlation / Roof Matrix
6. Weighted Importance of TRs
7. Competitive Benchmarks
8. Target Values

**Roof Explanation (1.5 marks):** The **Roof** is the **Correlation Matrix** placed on top of the technical requirements columns. It analyses the **inter-relationships between technical requirements themselves**. A strong positive correlation (⊕) means improving one TR automatically improves another. A strong negative correlation (⊖) means improving one TR worsens another — engineering must then find a balance or trade-off. The roof helps engineers **identify design conflicts early** before detailed design begins.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

#### **Question 3A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** Explain the concept of Quality Function Deployment (QFD) and describe the **four phases** of QFD deployment with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Construct the **House of Quality** for an electric kettle with **six customer requirements** and **five technical requirements**. Use the standard scoring convention (⊙ = 9, ○ = 3, △ = 1) and compute the **absolute weight** for each technical requirement. Interpret the top-ranked TR. *(7 marks)*

#### **Question 3B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **House of Quality** and describe each of its **eight components** in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A washing machine manufacturer collects VOC on seven customer attributes and identifies six technical responses. The importance ratings (out of 5) are: Wash quality = 5, Low noise = 4, Less water = 4, Fabric safety = 5, Speed = 3, Low cost = 4, Energy saving = 5. Build the relationship matrix using the provided symbol table, and compute the **weighted importance** for each TR. State the **ranking** of TRs. *(7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 Model Step-by-Step Solution for Question 3A(b)

**Given Data:**

| # | Customer Requirement | IR (1–5) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Boils water fast | 5 |
| 2 | Energy efficient | 4 |
| 3 | Easy to pour | 4 |
| 4 | Cool-touch handle | 5 |
| 5 | Auto shut-off | 5 |
| 6 | Affordable price | 4 |

**Technical Requirements:** A = Power (W), B = Insulation (mm), C = Spout angle (°), D = Sensor accuracy (°C), E = Body finish (index).

**Relationship Matrix (engineer-judged):**

| CR \ TR | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ⊙ | ○ | △ |  |  |
| 2 | ⊙ | △ |  | △ |  |
| 3 |  |  | ⊙ |  | △ |
| 4 | △ | ⊙ |  |  | △ |
| 5 | △ |  |  | ⊙ |  |
| 6 |  |  |  |  | ⊙ |

**Step 1: Convert symbols to scores (⊙=9, ○=3, △=1).**

| CR \ TR | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 9 | 3 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 9 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 9 | 0 | 1 |
| 4 | 1 | 9 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 5 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 9 | 0 |
| 6 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 9 |

**Step 2: Compute Weighted Importance using $TW_j = \sum IR_i \cdot R_{ij}$.**

$$
TW_A = (5)(9) + (4)(9) + (4)(0) + (5)(1) + (5)(1) + (4)(0) = 45 + 36 + 0 + 5 + 5 + 0 = 91
$$

$$
TW_B = (5)(3) + (4)(1) + (4)(0) + (5)(9) + (5)(0) + (4)(0) = 15 + 4 + 0 + 45 + 0 + 0 = 64
$$

$$
TW_C = (5)(1) + (4)(0) + (4)(9) + (5)(0) + (5)(0) + (4)(0) = 5 + 0 + 36 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 41
$$

$$
TW_D = (5)(0) + (4)(1) + (4)(0) + (5)(0) + (5)(9) + (4)(0) = 0 + 4 + 0 + 0 + 45 + 0 = 49
$$

$$
TW_E = (5)(0) + (4)(0) + (4)(1) + (5)(1) + (5)(0) + (4)(9) = 0 + 0 + 4 + 5 + 0 + 36 = 45
$$

**Step 3: Compute Absolute Weight.**
$$
TW_{\text{total}} = 91 + 64 + 41 + 49 + 45 = 290
$$

| TR | $TW_j$ | $AW_j$ (%) | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|
| A — Power rating | 91 | 31.38 % | **1** |
| B — Insulation | 64 | 22.07 % | **2** |
| D — Sensor accuracy | 49 | 16.90 % | **3** |
| E — Body finish | 45 | 15.52 % | **4** |
| C — Spout angle | 41 | 14.14 % | **5** |

**Step 4: Engineering Interpretation (KTU-style 1-mark conclusion):**
Since **TR-A (Power rating)** has the highest absolute weight (≈ 31.4 %), the design team should optimise wattage first to satisfy the dominant customer demands of *fast boiling* and *energy efficiency*. **Spout angle (C)** receives least priority and can be standardised to reduce cost.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in QFD Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Scoring Convention** — Not stating ⊙ = 9, ○ = 3, △ = 1 explicitly. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Missing the Roof** — Many students explain only the centre relationship matrix and skip the correlation roof. **(−2 marks)**
> 3. **No Normalisation** — Writing raw weighted scores but skipping the absolute weight (percentage) column. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **No Interpretation** — Computing numbers but not stating *what engineering action* the ranking implies. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Confusing Four Phases with One Matrix** — QFD ≠ HOQ. Always mention all four phases in long answers. **(−2 marks)**
> 6. **Forgetting the Originator** — Not mentioning **Yoji Akao / Japan / Toyota** in definitions. **(−1 mark)**
> 7. **Wrong Equation Form** — Writing $TW_j = \sum R_{ij}$ without multiplying by importance ratings. **(−1 mark)**

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 60-Second Summary**

- **QFD = Voice of Customer → Engineering Requirements**, pioneered by **Yoji Akao (Japan)**.
- **Main tool** = **House of Quality (HOQ)**.
- **Four Phases:** Product Planning → Product Design → Process Planning → Production Planning.
- **HOQ Components (8):** CRs, Importance, TRs, Relationship Matrix, Roof, Weighted Importance, Benchmark, Target Values.
- **Scoring:** ⊙ = 9, ○ = 3, △ = 1, blank = 0.
- **Correlation Roof:** ⊕ = Strong Positive, ⊖ = Strong Negative (conflict detection).
- **Key Equations:**
  - $TW_j = \sum_i IR_i \cdot R_{ij}$
  - $AW_j = (TW_j / \sum TW_j) \times 100\%$
  - $SWS_i = IR_i \times SP_i$ (with sales points).
- **Benefits:** Reduced design changes, faster time-to-market, cross-functional alignment.
- **Limitation:** Time-consuming initial setup; requires accurate VOC data.
- **Where used:** Automotive, software, healthcare, aerospace, service industries.
- **Draw neat diagrams** — examiners reward **structured HOQ diagrams** in long answers.
- **Always interpret** the final ranking with an engineering recommendation.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benefits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT636 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Benefits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Benefits of TQM (Total Quality Management):** The measurable and non-measurable improvements — spanning **customer satisfaction**, **operational efficiency**, **employee morale**, **cost reduction**, **competitive advantage**, and **societal welfare** — that accrue to an organization and its stakeholders as a direct outcome of the systematic implementation of Total Quality Management principles, tools, and culture.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the *Benefits* of TQM are classified into the following canonical categories, all of which are direct exam-favorite areas:

| # | Benefit Category | Stakeholder Focus |
|---|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Customer Benefits** | External end-users |
| 2 | **Employee Benefits** | Internal workforce |
| 3 | **Organization Benefits** | Company/Enterprise |
| 4 | **Supplier Benefits** | Supply chain partners |
| 5 | **Societal Benefits** | Community/Nation |
| 6 | **Strategic / Long-term Benefits** | Future sustainability |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Garden Analogy:** Imagine a garden where a gardener (TQM philosophy) tends to the soil, plants, and ecosystem holistically — rather than just watering one plant. The garden thrives when *every* element is healthy: the soil (employees), the plants (processes), the bees (suppliers), and the visitors (customers). The "Benefits" of TQM are the **fruits, blossoms, and the lush landscape** that emerge naturally when the entire ecosystem is nurtured through continuous improvement (Kaizen), proper tools (the 7 QC tools), and a quality-first mindset.

**Plain English:** When a company does TQM *right*, *everybody wins* — customers get better products, employees enjoy better working conditions, the company earns more profit, suppliers get stable long-term contracts, and society benefits from responsible business practices.

## 1.3 KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Focus:** Students must be able to (a) list benefits under each stakeholder category, (b) differentiate between **Tangible** (quantitative) and **Intangible** (qualitative) benefits, and (c) explain with examples how TQM creates a **competitive advantage** in the marketplace. Direct 3-mark and 14-mark questions on "Benefits of TQM" appear almost every semester.

**Key Standard Metrics** used to measure TQM benefits (must know for KTU):
- **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)**
- **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** — typically **15–40%** of sales revenue in poor-quality firms
- **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** — Six Sigma target: **3.4 DPMO**
- **Employee Turnover Rate** — TQM firms aim for **<10%**
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — Target: **>95%**
- **Return on Quality (ROQ)** — the financial return per rupee invested in quality
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of TQM Benefits — Structured Logic

### Pillar 1: Benefits to Customers (External Stakeholders)
TQM's first and foremost beneficiary is the **customer**. The philosophy holds that *"quality is what the customer says it is."*

- **Improved Product/Service Quality:** Fewer defects, better reliability, longer product life.
- **Lower Total Cost of Ownership:** Although unit price may rise slightly, lifetime cost (maintenance, repair, downtime) drops sharply.
- **Improved Customer Satisfaction & Loyalty:** Delighted customers become repeat buyers and brand advocates.
- **Faster Response / Lead Time:** Streamlined processes shorten delivery cycles.
- **Better After-Sales Service:** Complaint resolution becomes systematic (e.g., through 8D reports).

### Pillar 2: Benefits to Employees (Internal Stakeholders)
Quality is built *by* people, *for* people. TQM recognizes the human capital dimension.

- **Higher Job Satisfaction:** Empowerment through Quality Circles (QCs) and Suggestion Schemes.
- **Better Working Conditions:** Safe, clean, ergonomically designed workplaces (5S foundation).
- **Lower Stress & Absenteeism:** Smooth processes reduce fire-fighting and rework pressure.
- **Skill Development:** Cross-functional training (Kaizen, 5 Whys, FMEA) enhances employability.
- **Sense of Ownership:** PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycles give employees a voice in improvement.
- **Reduced Turnover:** Engaged employees stay longer, saving recruitment costs.

### Pillar 3: Benefits to the Organization (Enterprise Level)
This is the most-examined pillar in KTU question papers.

- **Increased Productivity & Efficiency:** Less rework = more output per labor-hour.
- **Reduced Costs (COPQ Reduction):** Prevention is cheaper than appraisal, which is cheaper than failure.
- **Improved Profitability & ROI:** Higher quality → premium pricing OR larger market share.
- **Enhanced Competitive Advantage:** Quality becomes a *differentiator* (Porter's Generic Strategy).
- **Better Decision-Making:** Data-driven management (using the 7 QC tools + SPC).
- **Stronger Brand Image & Reputation:** Quality awards (Deming Prize, Malcolm Baldrige, CII-EXIM) boost credibility.
- **Improved Supplier Relationships:** Long-term partnerships replace adversarial buying.
- **Adaptability & Innovation:** Continuous improvement culture fosters R&D.
- **Reduced Waste & Environmental Impact:** Lean principles minimize scrap and emissions.

### Pillar 4: Benefits to Suppliers
TQM extends upstream through the supply chain.

- **Long-term, Stable Contracts:** Less price-shopping, more partnership.
- **Clear Specifications:** Reduces ambiguity and rejection rates.
- **Joint Improvement Programs:** Supplier Kaizen, vendor development.
- **Faster Payments & Fair Terms:** Trust-based relationships.
- **Capacity-Building Assistance:** Training from buyer to supplier (e.g., Toyota's supplier development model).

### Pillar 5: Benefits to Society
Quality has a macro-level spillover effect.

- **Safer Products:** Reduces accidents, injuries, and product recalls.
- **Environmental Protection:** Less industrial waste, lower carbon footprint (ISO 14000 alignment).
- **Economic Development:** Higher employment, better wages, stronger exports.
- **Higher Standard of Living:** Affordable, reliable goods elevate consumption patterns.
- **Ethical Business Practices:** Transparency, fair trade, anti-corruption.

### Pillar 6: Strategic / Long-Term Benefits
- **Organizational Learning:** Knowledge is institutionalized (e.g., through SOPs, SOP libraries).
- **Survivability in Recessions:** Lean, high-quality firms weather downturns better (proven in 2008 & 2020).
- **Global Competitiveness:** TQM is a passport to international markets (especially EU, Japan, USA).
- **Stakeholder Trust:** Investors, regulators, and communities trust quality-certified firms.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **quantitative metrics** examiners love to test in numerical sub-parts. Memorize the formulas and their typical target values.

| Metric | Formula | Typical TQM Target | Stakeholder |
|--------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| **COPQ** (Cost of Poor Quality) | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Prevention Cost}$ | 2.5%–5% of sales | Organization |
| **DPMO** (Defects per Million Opportunities) | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}$ | 3.4 (Six Sigma) | Customer/Org |
| **Sigma Level (Z)** | $\text{Z} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{2\sigma}$ (for two-sided specs) | 6$\sigma$ = World Class | Process |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units passing inspection first time}}{\text{Total units produced}} \times 100$ | $>$95% | Customer |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_{i}$ | Maximize | Process |
| **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** | $\text{CSI} = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Importance} \times \text{Performance})}{\sum \text{Importance}} \times 100$ | $>$85% | Customer |
| **Employee Turnover Rate** | $\text{Turnover} = \dfrac{\text{Employees leaving}}{\text{Average headcount}} \times 100$ | $<$10% | Employee |
| **Return on Quality (ROQ)** | $\text{ROQ} = \dfrac{\text{Profit gain from quality initiatives}}{\text{Cost of quality initiatives}}$ | $>$1.0 (positive ROI) | Organization |
| **Pareto 80/20 Rule** | 80% of problems come from 20% of causes | Vital few vs. trivial many | All |
| **Kaizen Savings** | $\text{Kaizen Savings} = (\text{Cost before} - \text{Cost after}) \times \text{Annual volume}$ | Continuous positive | Organization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe Symbol Alert:** In your answer scripts, never write absolute values like `|x|` using the vertical pipe inside markdown tables — it breaks rendering. Use LaTeX `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert` instead.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern engineering firms, TQM benefits translate directly into:
- **Manufacturing:** Lower rejection rates, higher OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness).
- **Software:** Lower bug counts, faster releases, better user experience (Agile + TQM).
- **Construction:** Fewer structural defects, lower rework costs, safer sites.
- **Healthcare:** Lower hospital-acquired infections, shorter patient stays (Studer Group model).
- **Service Industry:** Reduced call-handling time, higher NPS (Net Promoter Score).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Benefits of TQM" is a *qualitative-management* topic, the "derivations" here are **logical-argument derivations** and **quantitative example calculations** that the KTU board expects in 14-mark analytical answers.

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Tangible vs. Intangible Benefits

| Dimension | Tangible Benefits (Quantifiable) | Intangible Benefits (Qualitative) |
|-----------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| **Customer** | Lower warranty claims, higher repeat-purchase rate | Brand loyalty, word-of-mouth advocacy |
| **Employee** | Lower turnover cost, higher productivity index | Pride of workmanship, morale |
| **Organization** | Higher profit margin, lower COPQ, higher market share | Reputation, culture, goodwill |
| **Supplier** | Higher order volume, longer contract tenure | Trust, mutual growth |
| **Society** | Lower environmental tax, higher employment rate | Safer products, ethical conduct |

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — COPQ Calculation

**Problem:** A manufacturing firm reports the following annual quality costs:
- Prevention Cost = ₹5,00,000
- Appraisal Cost = ₹3,00,000
- Internal Failure Cost = ₹8,00,000
- External Failure Cost = ₹12,00,000
- Total Sales Revenue = ₹2,00,00,000

**Find:** (a) Total COPQ, (b) COPQ as a % of sales, (c) If TQM reduces External Failure Cost by 60% and Internal Failure Cost by 40% (while Prevention rises by 50% and Appraisal rises by 20%), compute the new COPQ and % change.

### Solution:

**(a) Total COPQ (Before TQM):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ}_{\text{before}} &= \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 28{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) COPQ as % of Sales:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{COPQ} &= \frac{\text{COPQ}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{28{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 14\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This indicates a **poor-quality firm** (ideal TQM range: 2.5%–5%).

**(c) After TQM Implementation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New Prevention} &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 1.50 = 7{,}50{,}000 \\
\text{New Appraisal} &= 3{,}00{,}000 \times 1.20 = 3{,}60{,}000 \\
\text{New Internal Failure} &= 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 = 4{,}80{,}000 \\
\text{New External Failure} &= 12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 = 4{,}80{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ}_{\text{after}} &= 7{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}60{,}000 + 4{,}80{,}000 + 4{,}80{,}000 \\
&= 20{,}70{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Absolute Savings} &= 28{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}70{,}000 = 7{,}30{,}000 \text{ INR} \\
\text{\% Reduction in COPQ} &= \frac{7{,}30{,}000}{28{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 26.07\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation for KTU Answer:**
> TQM yields a **26.07% reduction in COPQ**, even though prevention and appraisal costs rise. This is the classic **Juran's Cost-of-Quality trade-off** — investing more in *prevention* and *appraisal* drastically reduces *failure* costs, producing a net positive ROI. This is a **direct exam-favorite calculation**.

## 3.3 Logical Derivation: Why TQM Benefits Compound Over Time

The **Pareto + Kaizen** feedback loop creates a *compounding benefit*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Benefit}_{t+1} &= \text{Benefit}_{t} \times (1 + r_{\text{kaizen}}) - \text{Decay}_{t} \\
\text{where} \quad r_{\text{kaizen}} &= \text{continuous improvement rate (typically 5\%–15\% per year)} \\
\text{Decay}_{t} &= \text{process drift without SPC control}
\end{aligned}
$$

When **SPC (Statistical Process Control)** is deployed, $\text{Decay}_{t} \to 0$, and benefits grow **exponentially**:

$$
\text{Benefit}_{n} = \text{Benefit}_{0} \times (1 + r)^{n}
$$

This is why Deming's 14 Points and Juran's Quality Trilogy all emphasize *continuity* — TQM benefits are **not one-shot gains** but **cumulative dividends**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"List any six benefits of TQM to the organization."**

**Model Answer (6 points, ~80 words):**
The benefits of TQM to the organization are: (i) **Increased productivity** through reduced rework and waste, (ii) **Lower Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** via prevention-focused spending, (iii) **Improved profitability and ROI** from premium pricing or larger market share, (iv) **Enhanced competitive advantage** positioning quality as a differentiator, (v) **Stronger brand image and reputation** through quality awards and certifications, and (vi) **Better supplier relationships** built on long-term partnerships. Collectively, these benefits create **sustainable organizational growth** and stakeholder trust.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Distinguish between Tangible and Intangible benefits of TQM with two examples each."**

**Model Answer (in table form, ~70 words):**

| Aspect | Tangible Benefits | Intangible Benefits |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Nature | Quantifiable, measurable in ₹ or % | Qualitative, perceptual |
| Example 1 | Reduction in COPQ from 14% to 8% of sales | Improved employee morale |
| Example 2 | Increase in First Pass Yield from 80% to 96% | Enhanced brand reputation |
| Measurement | Statistical & financial metrics | Surveys, observations, culture audits |

Tangible benefits justify ROI of TQM to top management, while intangible benefits create **lasting competitive moats**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **benefits of TQM to customers and employees**. How do these benefits reinforce each other in a quality-driven organization?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A firm has the following quality costs: Prevention = ₹4,00,000, Appraisal = ₹2,50,000, Internal Failure = ₹6,00,000, External Failure = ₹9,50,000. Annual sales = ₹1,50,00,000. (i) Calculate the COPQ and COPQ%. (ii) After TQM, prevention rises by 40%, appraisal by 25%, internal failure falls by 50%, and external failure falls by 70%. Compute the new COPQ and the **% savings achieved**. Comment on Juran's principle illustrated.

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Benefits to Customers and Employees:**

**Benefits to Customers (4 key points):**
1. **Higher Product/Service Quality:** Fewer defects, greater reliability, longer product life — directly resulting from process control (SPC) and employee skill development.
2. **Lower Total Cost of Ownership:** Customers spend less on repairs, maintenance, and downtime even if the initial price is slightly higher.
3. **Improved Satisfaction and Loyalty:** Quick response to complaints, customized offerings, and consistent quality build emotional and rational loyalty.
4. **Faster Delivery & Better Service:** Streamlined value streams (Lean) reduce lead time.

**Benefits to Employees (4 key points):**
1. **Higher Job Satisfaction:** Empowerment through Quality Circles, suggestion schemes, and Kaizen teams.
2. **Skill Enhancement:** Cross-functional training in problem-solving tools (7 QC tools, FMEA).
3. **Sense of Ownership & Pride:** PDCA engagement, recognition programs.
4. **Better Working Conditions:** 5S, safety, ergonomics, lower stress from fire-fighting.

**Reinforcement Loop (Mutual Reinforcement):**
> Empowered, skilled employees (internal benefit) → produce better products (external customer benefit) → customers reward firm with loyalty and positive word-of-mouth → firm earns higher revenue → re-invests in employee welfare and training → cycle compounds. This is the **virtuous cycle of TQM**, central to Deming's Chain Reaction: *Improve quality →降低成本→ productivity rises → capture market → stay in business → provide jobs.*

#### **Part (b) — COPQ Numerical:**

**Step 1: Original COPQ**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ}_{\text{before}} &= 4{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}50{,}000 \\
&= 22{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\% \text{COPQ} = \frac{22{,}00{,}000}{1{,}50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 14.67\%
$$

**Step 2: Post-TQM Costs**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Prevention}_{new} &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 1.40 = 5{,}60{,}000 \\
\text{Appraisal}_{new} &= 2{,}50{,}000 \times 1.25 = 3{,}12{,}500 \\
\text{Internal Failure}_{new} &= 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 = 3{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{External Failure}_{new} &= 9{,}50{,}000 \times 0.30 = 2{,}85{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: New COPQ**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ}_{\text{after}} &= 5{,}60{,}000 + 3{,}12{,}500 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}85{,}000 \\
&= 14{,}57{,}500 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: % Savings**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= 22{,}00{,}000 - 14{,}57{,}500 = 7{,}42{,}500 \\
\% \text{Savings} &= \frac{7{,}42{,}500}{22{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 33.75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Juran's Principle Illustrated:** *"Optimal quality is achieved at the point where the sum of prevention + appraisal costs equals the savings from reduced failure costs."* The data confirms: even though prevention and appraisal rose by ₹2,22,500 combined, failure costs fell by ₹10,65,000 — yielding a net saving of ₹7,42,500. This is the **Gold Mine of Prevention Cost** concept.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **benefits of TQM to the organization and to society**. How do these benefits create a long-term sustainable competitive advantage?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Six-Sigma project records the following: 12,500 units produced, 380 defects found, each unit has 4 defect opportunities. (i) Calculate DPMO. (ii) Determine the **Sigma Level** using the standard conversion table. (iii) Comment on the quality status.

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Organization & Society Benefits:**

**Benefits to Organization (5 key points):**
1. **Increased Productivity:** Smooth processes eliminate non-value-adding activities (Lean + TQM).
2. **Reduced Costs (COPQ):** As demonstrated in Juran's model, prevention spending yields high ROI.
3. **Enhanced Competitive Advantage:** Quality becomes a *brand promise*, not just a feature.
4. **Improved Decision-Making:** Data-driven tools (SPC, Pareto, Ishikawa) replace gut-feel.
5. **Stronger Innovation Pipeline:** Kaizen culture breeds incremental and breakthrough innovation.
6. **Better Investor & Stakeholder Confidence:** ISO/Baldrige-certified firms attract capital easily.

**Benefits to Society (4 key points):**
1. **Safer Products:** Reduced accidents, recalls, and product-related injuries.
2. **Environmental Protection:** Less waste, lower emissions — aligned with ISO 14000 sustainability.
3. **Higher Employment & Wages:** Productive firms pay better and hire more.
4. **Ethical Business Conduct:** Transparency, fair trade, anti-bribery culture.

**Long-term Sustainable Competitive Advantage:**
The benefits compound via three reinforcing mechanisms: **(a) Cost Leadership** (lower COPQ = lower price or higher margin), **(b) Differentiation** (premium quality justifies premium pricing), and **(c) Focus** (niche quality leadership). Combined with **organizational learning**, these create a **Porter's Diamond effect** where the firm cannot be easily replicated by competitors.

#### **Part (b) — Six-Sigma DPMO Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Units produced (N) = 12,500
- Defects (D) = 380
- Opportunities per unit (O) = 4
- Total Opportunities = N × O = 50,000

**Step 1: DPMO Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{D}{N \times O} \times 10^{6} \\
&= \frac{380}{12{,}500 \times 4} \times 10^{6} \\
&= \frac{380}{50{,}000} \times 10^{6} \\
&= 7600 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Sigma Level Conversion**

Using the standard Six-Sigma conversion table:

| DPMO | Sigma Level |
|------|-------------|
| 3.4 | 6.0 |
| 233 | 5.0 |
| 6210 | 4.0 |
| 66807 | 3.0 |

By interpolation, **DPMO = 7600 ≈ 4.0 Sigma Level** (industry average is 3–4 sigma; world-class is 6 sigma).

**Step 3: Quality Status Comment**

> The process is performing at approximately **4-sigma level**, which is the **industry average** but **far from world-class (6-sigma)**. The defect rate of 7600 DPMO means about **0.76% defects**, translating to thousands of defective units annually. The firm should launch a **DMAIC project** to identify root causes (using Pareto, Ishikawa) and push towards 5-sigma, then 6-sigma. The gap to 3.4 DPMO represents enormous **latent benefit potential** in cost savings, customer satisfaction, and competitive positioning.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in "Benefits of TQM" Questions:**
> 1. **Writing generic statements without examples** — Examiners allocate marks for *specific* benefits (e.g., "Lower COPQ from 14% to 5%") not vague platitudes ("improves quality").
> 2. **Confusing benefits with principles** — TQM *principles* (customer focus, leadership) are NOT the same as *benefits* (satisfaction, lower cost). Mixing them costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting all 5 stakeholder groups** — A 14-mark answer must cover at least **customers, employees, organization, suppliers, and society**. Missing two groups = -4 marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning intangible benefits** — Many students list only tangible ones. Examiners expect **both** with a clear distinction.
> 5. **Skipping numerical proof in analytical questions** — Always include a **COPQ or DPMO calculation** to anchor the conceptual answer in data.
> 6. **Mishandling LaTeX pipes in tables** — Writing `|x|` inside a markdown table breaks it on screen. Use `\lvert x \rvert`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Benefits of TQM (Module 3):**

- **Six beneficiary groups:** Customers, Employees, Organization, Suppliers, Society, and Long-term Strategic Stakeholders.
- **Two benefit types:** **Tangible** (measurable in ₹/%) and **Intangible** (qualitative, cultural).
- **Core Organization Benefits (Top 5 for exams):** Higher productivity, Lower COPQ, Higher profit/ROI, Competitive advantage, Better brand image.
- **Core Customer Benefits (Top 4):** Better quality, Lower lifetime cost, Higher satisfaction, Faster delivery.
- **Core Employee Benefits (Top 4):** Job satisfaction, Skill development, Sense of ownership, Lower turnover.
- **Juran's Principle:** Prevention + Appraisal costs are investments; Failure costs are penalties. Net savings = TQM benefit.
- **Deming's Chain Reaction:** Quality → Costs↓ → Productivity↑ → Market Share↑ → Jobs↑ → Sustainability.
- **Six-Sigma Target:** **3.4 DPMO** = **6-sigma** = world-class.
- **COPQ Healthy Range:** **2.5%–5%** of sales. Above 10% = poor quality management.
- **First Pass Yield Target:** **>95%** in TQM firms.
- **Turnover Target:** **<10%** in quality-driven organizations.
- **Three Strategic Benefits:** Cost Leadership, Differentiation, Organizational Learning.
- **Real-World Benchmarks:** Toyota, Tata Steel (Deming Prize winner), Wipro (Baldrige), Maruti Suzuki.
- **Always pair your answer with:** A **numerical illustration** (COPQ or DPMO) + a **real company example** for full 14-mark credit.

> **End of Module 3 — Topic: Benefits | PEICT636 Total Quality Management | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TPM and FMEA: Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) Concept and Stages of FMEA Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT636 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** TPM and FMEA: Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) Concept and Stages of FMEA Concept # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) — The Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)** is a **holistic, company-wide, proactive and preventive maintenance system** that aims to maximize the operational effectiveness of production equipment by eliminating breakdowns, defects, accidents, and waste through the participation and ownership of *all employees* — from top management to shop-floor operators. **Origin & Authority:** TPM was formalized by **Seiichi Nakajima** (Japan Institute of Plant Maintenance, JIPM) in **1971**, drawing heavily from **Preventive Maintenance** and the principles of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**. It is also referred to as the *"Nakajima Method"* in industry literature. **KTU Board Definition (verbatim from module syllabus):** > *"TPM is the productive maintenance carried out by all employees through small group activities, aiming at zero breakdowns, zero defects, and zero accidents."* ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Family Car vs. The Company Machine" > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Analogy:** Think of TPM like maintaining your personal car for 20 years. You don't just visit the mechanic when the engine fails (breakdown maintenance). You also don't only service it on a fixed calendar (preventive maintenance). Instead, you (the *operator*) check the oil daily, you (the *technician*) inspect belts monthly, and you (the *family*) ensure no one over-revvs the engine. **Every family member has a role.** TPM applies this same *collective ownership* to industrial machines: operators, engineers, managers — everyone participates. ### The Three Zeros of TPM | Zero | Meaning | KTU Significance | |:----:|:--------|:-----------------| | **Zero Breakdowns** | No unplanned machine stoppages | Maximizes OEE Availability | | **Zero Defects** | No defective products produced | Drives Quality pillar of OEE | | **Zero Accidents** | No workplace injuries | Sustains morale & continuity | ### Physical/Industrial Constants in TPM > [!IMPORTANT] > - **World-Class OEE Benchmark = 85%** (Nakajima standard) > - **TPM target Loss Reduction = 90% in 3 years** (JIPM award criteria) > - **Autonomous Maintenance Step-up duration = ~12 months per machine** --- ## 1.2 FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) — The Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **FMEA** is a **systematic, step-by-step analytical methodology** used to identify, evaluate, prioritize, and eliminate (or control) potential failure modes of a product, process, or service **before** they reach the customer. **Two Industry Variants Recognized by KTU:** 1. **DFMEA — Design FMEA** (product design stage) 2. **PFMEA — Process FMEA** (manufacturing process stage) ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Pilot's Pre-Flight Checklist" > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Analogy:** Before a pilot takes off, they don't just "fly and hope." They run through a structured mental list: *"What if engine 1 fails on takeoff? What if the landing gear doesn't deploy?"* They score each scenario by *how bad* (severity), *how likely* (occurrence), and *how easily detected before crash* (detectability). FMEA is exactly this — but for engineering products and processes. It is a *systematic imagination of failure* before it happens. ### Core FMEA Metrics (Bolded for KTU emphasis) > [!IMPORTANT] > - **S = Severity (1–10)** — How serious is the failure to the customer? > - **O = Occurrence (1–10)** — How frequently does the failure happen? > - **D = Detection (1–10)** — How hard is it to detect *before* reaching customer? > - **RPN = Risk Priority Number = S × O × D** (older method) > - **AP = Action Priority (H/M/L)** — newer AIAG-VDA 2019 approach --- ## 1.3 Why TPM and FMEA Sit Together in KTU Module 4 > [!NOTE] > TPM provides the **operational/proactive maintenance framework** that *prevents* failures, while FMEA provides the **analytical/risk-prioritization tool** that *predicts and controls* failures. Together, they form the **"Prevention + Prediction"** backbone of Total Quality Management in any manufacturing or service organization. KTU tests this integration frequently in Part B questions. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 TPM — Deep Theoretical Framework ### 2.1.1 The Eight Pillars of TPM (Nakajima Model) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU frequently asks: *"Explain the 8 pillars of TPM."* This is a **direct 7-mark Part B** topic. Memorize all 8 — no pillar can be skipped. ``` ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ TPM = MAINTENANCE PILLARS (4) + DEVELOPMENT PILLARS (4) │ │ ┌──────────────────────────┐ ┌────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ Pillar 1: Autonomous Mx │ │ Pillar 5: Early Mgmt of │ │ │ │ Pillar 2: Planned Mx │ │ New Products │ │ │ │ Pillar 3: Quality Mx │ │ Pillar 6: Administrative │ │ │ │ Pillar 4: Education/Tng │ │ Pillar 7: Office TPM │ │ │ └──────────────────────────┘ │ Pillar 8: Safety, Health │ │ │ │ & Environment (SHE) │ │ │ └────────────────────────────┘ │ └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` | # | Pillar | Core Idea | KTU One-Liner | |:-:|:-------|:----------|:--------------| | 1 | **Autonomous Maintenance (AM)** | Operators own daily cleaning, lubrication, inspection | "Operators maintain, not just operate" | | 2 | **Planned Maintenance (PM)** | Time-based & condition-based preventive schedules | "Zero unplanned breakdowns" | | 3 | **Quality Maintenance (QM)** | Defect prevention through condition monitoring | "Zero defects from machines" | | 4 | **Education & Training** | Multi-skill development across hierarchy | "Train everyone on everything" | | 5 | **Early Management of New Products** | Design machines easy to maintain from Day 1 | "LMTD — Life Cycle Maintenance Design" | | 6 | **Administrative TPM** | Office/support functions adopt TPM tools | "TPM beyond shop-floor" | | 7 | **Office TPM** | Streamline information & documentation flow | "Zero paperwork waste" | | 8 | **Safety, Health & Environment** | Zero accidents & zero pollution | "Sustainable TPM" | ### 2.1.2 OEE — Overall Equipment Effectiveness (The Heart of TPM) > [!IMPORTANT] > **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality** > This is the single most-tested TPM formula in KTU. It identifies the *Six Big Losses*. ### 2.1.3 The Six Big Losses (Nakajima's Loss Classification) ``` ┌───────────────────────┐ │ SIX BIG LOSSES │ │ (3 Categories) │ └───────────┬───────────┘ │ ┌───────────────────────┼────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ AVAILABILITY PERFORMANCE QUALITY │ │ │ ┌────┴────┐ ┌────┴────┐ ┌────┴────┐ │Equipment│ │Reduced │ │Process │ │Failure │ │Speed │ │Defects │ ① ──────── │ ④ │ │ ⑤ │ │Setup & │ │Idle & │ │Startup │ │Adjust. │ │Minor │ │Defects │ ② ──────── │Stops │ │ ⑥ │ ③ ──────── └─────────┘ └─────────┘ ``` | Loss Category | Loss # | Loss Name | Example | |:--------------|:------:|:----------|:--------| | **Availability Losses** | ① | Equipment Failure | Motor burn-out | | | ② | Setup & Adjustment | Changeover time | | **Performance Losses** | ③ | Idling & Minor Stops | Sensor trips | | | ④ | Reduced Speed | Operator runs slow | | **Quality Losses** | ⑤ | Process Defects | Wrong dimension | | | ⑥ | Startup Defects | First-piece rejection | --- ## 2.2 FMEA — Deep Theoretical Framework ### 2.2.1 The 10 Steps of a Standard FMEA (AIAG-VDA 2019 Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU tests the **stages of FMEA** as either Part A (3 marks) or Part B (7–14 marks). Below is the *canonical* order accepted in KTU valuation. | Stage | Activity | Output | |:-:|:---------|:-------| | **1. Scope Definition** | Define boundary, system, customer | FMEA Scope Document | | **2. Team Assembly** | Cross-functional experts | FMEA Team Charter | | **3. Failure Identification** | List *all* potential failure modes | Failure Mode List | | **4. Effect Analysis** | What happens to customer/process | Effects Description | | **5. Cause Analysis** | Root cause (5-Whys, Ishikawa) | Cause List | | **6. Severity (S) Rating** | Score 1–10 | Severity column | | **7. Occurrence (O) Rating** | Score 1–10 | Occurrence column | | **8. Detection (D) Rating** | Score 1–10 | Detection column | | **9. Risk Priority (RPN/AP)** | Calculate & rank | Action Priority list | | **10. Actions & Verification** | Reduce S, O, or D; re-score | Updated RPN / AP | ### 2.2.2 RPN Computation Logic > [!IMPORTANT] > **RPN = Severity × Occurrence × Detection** > Range: **1 (lowest risk) → 1000 (highest risk)** > Industry threshold for action: **RPN ≥ 100** *or* **Severity ≥ 9** --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The table below is your **exam-day cheat sheet**. Use `\vert` (not `\|`) for absolute value to preserve table integrity. | # | Formula / Concept | Symbols & Meaning | KTU Use Case | |:-:|:------------------|:------------------|:-------------| | 1 | **OEE = A × P × Q** | $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality | TPM — Loss Analysis | | 2 | **Availability (A)** | $\dfrac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} \times 100\%$ | OEE numerator-1 | | 3 | **Performance (P)** | $\dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}} \times 100\%$ | OEE numerator-2 | | 4 | **Quality (Q)** | $\dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}} \times 100\%$ | OEE numerator-3 | | 5 | **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) | $\dfrac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Reliability metric | | 6 | **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) | $\dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | Maintainability metric | | 7 | **RPN** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$, where $1 \le S,O,D \le 10$ | FMEA Risk Ranking | | 8 | **RPN Threshold** | Action if $RPN \ge 100$ | FMEA Trigger | | 9 | **AP (Action Priority)** | $\text{AP} \in \{H, M, L\}$ (High/Medium/Low) | New AIAG-VDA method | | 10 | **Six Big Losses** | 2 Availability + 2 Performance + 2 Quality | OEE Loss Tree | | 11 | **MTBF − MTTR = Availability Time** | (within one cycle) | Reliability vs Maintainability | | 12 | **TPM Target** | $OEE \ge 85\%$ = World Class | JIPM Standard | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **TPM** powers the "Zero Defect, Zero Breakdown" culture at **Toyota, Hyundai, Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki**. - **FMEA** is **mandatory** in **automotive (IATF 16949)**, **aerospace (AS9100)**, **medical devices (ISO 13485)** and **semiconductor manufacturing** as a regulatory compliance tool. - Combined, TPM + FMEA is the backbone of **Industry 4.0 predictive maintenance** systems. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: OEE from the Six Big Losses We begin from the **first principles** of equipment productivity. ### Step 1 — Define Total Calendar Time (T) $$T_{\text{calendar}} = 24 \text{ hours/day} \times \text{Number of Operating Days}$$ > *(Used as the universal time base for availability computation.)* ### Step 2 — Subtract Non-Operating Time $$T_{\text{planned}} = T_{\text{calendar}} - T_{\text{non-operating}}$$ Where $T_{\text{non-operating}}$ includes weekends, holidays, and scheduled shutdowns. ### Step 3 — Subtract Downtime Losses (Loss ① + Loss ②) $$T_{\text{operating}} = T_{\text{planned}} - T_{\text{downtime}}$$ Where $T_{\text{downtime}}$ captures *Equipment Failure* (Loss ①) and *Setup/Adjustment* (Loss ②). ### Step 4 — Compute Availability Component $$\text{Availability} = \frac{T_{\text{operating}}}{T_{\text{planned}}} \times 100\%$$ ### Step 5 — Compute Performance Component (uses Loss ③ + Loss ④) $$\text{Performance} = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces Produced}}{T_{\text{operating}}} \times 100\%$$ This factor accounts for *Idling/Minor Stops* (Loss ③) and *Reduced Speed* (Loss ④). ### Step 6 — Compute Quality Component (uses Loss ⑤ + Loss ⑥) $$\text{Quality} = \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}} \times 100\%$$ ### Step 7 — Multiply All Three Components $$\boxed{OEE = A \times P \times Q = \frac{T_{\text{operating}}}{T_{\text{planned}}} \times \frac{N_{\text{ideal}}}{T_{\text{operating}}} \times \frac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}}}$$ Where $N$ denotes *Number of pieces*. ### Worked KTU-Style Numerical Example > **Problem (KTU Typical 7-Mark):** > A CNC machine operates 8 hours/day for 25 days/month. > - Scheduled downtime = 30 hours/month > - Ideal cycle time = 1.2 minutes/piece > - Total pieces produced = 8,000 > - Defective pieces = 200 > Compute the OEE. **Solution:** $$\text{Availability} = \frac{(8 \times 25) - 30}{8 \times 25} \times 100\% = \frac{170}{200} \times 100\% = 85\%$$ $$\text{Performance} = \frac{1.2 \times 8000}{(8 \times 25 - 30) \times 60} \times 100\%$$ The denominator $T_{\text{operating}} = 170$ hours $= 170 \times 60 = 10200$ minutes. $$\text{Performance} = \frac{9600}{10200} \times 100\% = 94.12\%$$ $$\text{Quality} = \frac{8000 - 200}{8000} \times 100\% = 97.5\%$$ $$\boxed{OEE = 0.85 \times 0.9412 \times 0.975 = 0.7798 = 77.98\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is below the 85% world-class benchmark — the machine needs **TPM intervention**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: RPN from Severity, Occurrence & Detection ### Step 1 — Tabulate Failure Data | Failure Mode | Effect | Cause | S | O | D | |:-------------|:-------|:------|:-:|:-:|:-:| | Bearing seizure | Machine stops | Lubrication failure | 8 | 4 | 5 | | Sensor drift | Wrong readings | Calibration loss | 7 | 6 | 3 | | Coolant leak | Process contamination | Hose aging | 9 | 5 | 4 | ### Step 2 — Apply the Multiplication Rule $$\text{RPN}_1 = S_1 \times O_1 \times D_1 = 8 \times 4 \times 5 = 160$$ $$\text{RPN}_2 = S_2 \times O_2 \times D_2 = 7 \times 6 \times 3 = 126$$ $$\text{RPN}_3 = S_3 \times O_3 \times D_3 = 9 \times 5 \times 4 = 180$$ ### Step 3 — Rank by Risk Priority $$\boxed{\text{RPN}_3 (180) > \text{RPN}_1 (160) > \text{RPN}_2 (126)}$$ ### Step 4 — Apply Action Rule > [!IMPORTANT] > **Action Trigger:** If $\text{RPN} \ge 100$ **OR** $\text{Severity} \ge 9$, immediate corrective action is required. Since $\text{RPN}_3 = 180 > 100$ **and** $S_3 = 9$, the **Coolant Leak** failure mode demands top priority — redesign the hose or add a leak sensor. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: TPM ↔ FMEA Integration > [!NOTE] > This matrix is **highly valued** in KTU Part B answers — examiners award full marks when you connect TPM's *proactive maintenance* with FMEA's *predictive analysis*. | TPM Element | FMEA Linkage | Combined Outcome | |:------------|:-------------|:-----------------| | Autonomous Maintenance (Pillar 1) | Operators detect failure modes early → lower **O** & **D** | RPN reduction | | Planned Maintenance (Pillar 2) | Scheduled tasks address high-**O** causes | MTBF ↑ | | Quality Maintenance (Pillar 3) | Targets process defects (S=10 cases) | S → lower through Poka-Yoke | | Early Mgmt (Pillar 5) | DFMEA executed at design stage | Built-in quality | | Education (Pillar 4) | Train FMEA teams on rating scales | Reliable S/O/D scoring | --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Reliability from MTBF and MTTR $$\text{Availability}_{\text{inherent}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$ This equation shows that **TPM** drives both: - **MTBF ↑** (through preventive maintenance) - **MTTR ↓** (through quick-changeover & training) The combined effect is a **higher Availability** — directly feeding into OEE. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) and state its three core objectives.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** TPM, formalized by **Seiichi Nakajima** in 1971, is a company-wide, proactive maintenance philosophy aimed at maximizing equipment effectiveness throughout the entire life of the machine. The **three core objectives** (the "Three Zeros") are: 1. **Zero Breakdowns** — eliminate unplanned equipment failures. 2. **Zero Defects** — prevent production of defective items from machines. 3. **Zero Accidents** — maintain a safe, accident-free workplace. > *Award 1 mark for definition; 2 marks for listing the three zeros with one-line explanation each.* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the Risk Priority Number (RPN) in FMEA? Write the formula and state the threshold for taking action.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** is a numerical score used in FMEA to prioritize failure modes based on their overall risk to the customer. $$\boxed{\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D}$$ Where: - $S$ = **Severity** rating (1 to 10) — seriousness of failure consequence - $O$ = **Occurrence** rating (1 to 10) — likelihood of failure happening - $D$ = **Detection** rating (1 to 10) — difficulty of detecting the failure before reaching the customer **Action Threshold:** Corrective action must be initiated if **$\text{RPN} \ge 100$** *or* if **$\text{Severity} \ge 9$**, regardless of RPN value. > *Award 1 mark for formula; 1 mark for parameter explanation; 1 mark for threshold.* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Examiner Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **one full question** of 14 marks from the Module 4 internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below — one focusing on TPM, one on FMEA. --- ### 🔵 Question A (14 Marks) — TPM Focus `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **Eight Pillars of TPM** with their significance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A bottling plant operates 2 shifts of 8 hours each, 30 days/month. The machine has: - Setup and breakdown losses = 60 hours/month - Ideal cycle time = 2 sec/bottle - Total bottles produced = 700,000 - Defective bottles = 7,000 Calculate the **OEE** and identify which loss category is most dominant. **(7 marks)** #### 📝 Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] The Eight Pillars of TPM, as defined by Seiichi Nakajima, are divided into **four Maintenance Pillars** and **four Development Pillars**: **Maintenance Pillars:** 1. **Autonomous Maintenance (AM):** Operators themselves perform daily cleaning, lubrication, inspection, and tightening of their machines. This instills ownership and early detection of abnormalities. *(1 mark)* 2. **Planned Maintenance (PM):** A systematic schedule of preventive and predictive maintenance designed to eliminate unplanned breakdowns. Uses MTBF/MTTR analysis. *(1 mark)* 3. **Quality Maintenance (QM):** Condition-monitoring techniques to detect deterioration that could lead to defects. Achieves "zero defects from machines" through Poka-Yoke and Statistical Process Control. *(1 mark)* 4. **Education and Training:** Multi-skill development — operators learn basic maintenance; engineers learn operating skills. Builds a flexible workforce. *(1 mark)* **Development Pillars:** 5. **Early Management of New Products:** Apply LMTD (Life-cycle Maintenance Time Design) so that new machines are easy to maintain from Day 1. *(1 mark)* 6. **Administrative TPM:** Apply TPM tools like 5S and Visual Management in non-production departments (HR, Finance, Purchase). *(0.75 mark)* 7. **Office TPM:** Eliminate paperwork waste and information delays. Improves information flow and decision-making speed. *(0.75 mark)* 8. **Safety, Health & Environment (SHE):** Zero-accident culture; sustainable practices; legal compliance. *(0.5 mark)* > *Award full marks when all 8 pillars are covered with at least one industrial application example.* --- #### 📝 Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given Data:** - Operating time = $2 \times 8 \times 30 = 480$ hours/month - Downtime losses (setup + breakdown) = 60 hours - Ideal cycle time = 2 sec/bottle - Total pieces = 700,000 - Defective pieces = 7,000 **Step 1 — Availability:** $$\text{Availability} = \frac{480 - 60}{480} \times 100\% = \frac{420}{480} \times 100\% = 87.5\%$$ **Step 2 — Performance:** Operating time = $420$ hours $= 420 \times 3600 = 1,512,000$ seconds. Ideal production = $\frac{1,512,000}{2} = 756,000$ bottles. $$\text{Performance} = \frac{756,000}{700,000} \times 100\% = 108\%$$ Since performance cannot exceed 100%, we cap at **100%** (or the calculation indicates *over-ideal cycle* assumption). *Correct approach (treating Performance as $\frac{\text{Total Pieces} \times \text{Ideal Cycle Time}}{\text{Operating Time}}$):* $$\text{Performance} = \frac{700,000 \times 2}{1,512,000} \times 100\% = \frac{1,400,000}{1,512,000} \times 100\% = 92.59\%$$ **Step 3 — Quality:** $$\text{Quality} = \frac{700,000 - 7,000}{700,000} \times 100\% = \frac{693,000}{700,000} \times 100\% = 99\%$$ **Step 4 — OEE:** $$\boxed{OEE = 0.875 \times 0.9259 \times 0.99 = 0.8021 = 80.21\%}$$ **Dominant Loss Identification:** - Availability gap from 100% = **12.5%** - Performance gap from 100% = **7.41%** - Quality gap from 100% = **1.0%** 👉 **Availability Losses (12.5%) are most dominant**, corresponding to Loss ① (Equipment Failure) and Loss ② (Setup/Adjustment). TPM recommendation: invest in **Autonomous Maintenance** + **Planned Maintenance** pillars. --- ### 🟢 Question B (14 Marks) — FMEA Focus `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **stages of FMEA** in detail with a suitable industrial example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The following failure modes have been identified for a hydraulic press in an automotive assembly. Compute RPN for each and recommend action priorities. | Failure Mode | Effect | S | O | D | |:-------------|:-------|:-:|:-:|:-:| | Seal leakage | Pressure drop | 8 | 6 | 4 | | Valve sticking | Slow actuation | 7 | 5 | 7 | | Hose burst | Sudden stoppage | 9 | 3 | 6 | | Pump cavitation | Noise + vibration | 6 | 7 | 5 | **(7 marks)** #### 📝 Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] The **stages of FMEA** (using the AIAG-VDA 2019 framework recognized by IATF 16949) are as follows: **Stage 1 — Define Scope and Identify Customers:** Clearly state the system boundary, the analysis depth, and the *internal/external customers* affected. *(0.5 mark)* **Stage 2 — Assemble the Cross-Functional Team:** Include design, manufacturing, quality, supplier, and service representatives. Team leader facilitates. *(0.5 mark)* **Stage 3 — Identify Potential Failure Modes:** For every component/function, ask *"How could it fail?"* Example: A brake pad could fail by *excessive wear, cracking, or delamination*. *(1 mark)* **Stage 4 — Determine Effects:** What does the customer experience? Example: *Reduced stopping distance → accident risk.* Effects are rated by **Severity (S)**. *(1 mark)* **Stage 5 — Determine Causes:** Apply **5-Whys** or **Ishikawa (Fishbone)** to find root causes. Example: *Excessive wear ← poor material ← supplier defect.* Causes are rated by **Occurrence (O)**. *(1 mark)* **Stage 6 — Determine Current Controls & Detection (D):** What existing checks (inspection, SPC, testing) detect the failure? A *low* D score = *easy* detection. *(0.75 mark)* **Stage 7 — Compute RPN:** $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$. Rank all failure modes. *(0.75 mark)* **Stage 8 — Take Action:** For high-RPN modes, define counter-measures (Poka-Yoke, redundancy, design change) to **reduce S, O, or D**. *(0.75 mark)* **Stage 9 — Re-score and Verify:** After action, recalculate RPN to confirm risk reduction. Document evidence. *(0.5 mark)* **Stage 10 — Continuous Review:** Re-evaluate whenever design/process changes occur. *(0.25 mark)* > *Award full marks when all stages are sequenced with one consistent industrial example throughout.* --- #### 📝 Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Calculate RPN for each failure mode:** $$\text{RPN}_{\text{Seal}} = 8 \times 6 \times 4 = 192$$ $$\text{RPN}_{\text{Valve}} = 7 \times 5 \times 7 = 245$$ $$\text{RPN}_{\text{Hose}} = 9 \times 3 \times 6 = 162$$ $$\text{RPN}_{\text{Cavitation}} = 6 \times 7 \times 5 = 210$$ **Step 2 — Rank by RPN (highest to lowest):** | Rank | Failure Mode | RPN | Action Priority | |:----:|:-------------|:---:|:----------------| | 1 | Valve sticking | **245** | **Highest** — Immediate action | | 2 | Pump cavitation | **210** | **High** — Action within 30 days | | 3 | Seal leakage | **192** | **High** — Action within 30 days | | 4 | Hose burst | **162** | **Medium** — Action within 90 days | **Step 3 — Apply Special Rule for Severity $\ge 9$:** The **Hose Burst** failure mode has $\text{Severity} = 9$. Even though its RPN is the lowest, the **severity rule mandates immediate action**, regardless of RPN value. **Step 4 — Recommended Actions:** 1. **Valve Sticking (RPN=245, top priority):** Switch to stainless-steel valve; add weekly cleaning schedule; install contamination sensor. 2. **Pump Cavitation (RPN=210):** Inspect suction line for air leaks; maintain proper fluid level. 3. **Seal Leakage (RPN=192):** Replace with double-lip seal; introduce TPM Planned Maintenance check. 4. **Hose Burst (S=9, mandatory action):** Use reinforced hose with burst sensor; install emergency shut-off valve. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the Severity Rule:** Many students rank purely by RPN and miss the mandatory action on $S \ge 9$ items (Hose Burst). **Lose 2 marks.** > 2. **Wrong formula units:** Ensure each rating is on the 1–10 scale; do not use percentage. > 3. **Omitting re-scoring:** After listing actions, the model answer should ideally re-state the *expected* new RPN. > 4. **No link to TPM:** FMEA actions should be tied to **Pillar 1 (Autonomous Maintenance)** or **Pillar 3 (Quality Maintenance)** for full conceptual integration marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** - ✅ **TPM Origin:** Seiichi Nakajima, JIPM, **1971**. - ✅ **Three Zeros:** Zero breakdowns, Zero defects, Zero accidents. - ✅ **Eight Pillars:** 4 Maintenance (AM, PM, QM, Education) + 4 Development (Early Mgmt, Admin, Office, SHE). - ✅ **OEE Formula:** $OEE = A \times P \times Q$. **World-class = 85%.** - ✅ **Six Big Losses:** 2 Availability + 2 Performance + 2 Quality. - ✅ **5S** is the **foundation** of TPM (Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke). - ✅ **FMEA Origin:** U.S. Military (MIL-STD-1629, 1949); now mandatory in IATF 16949. - ✅ **Two Main Types:** DFMEA (Design) and PFMEA (Process). - ✅ **RPN Formula:** $RPN = S \times O \times D$, range 1–1000. - ✅ **RPN Action Rule:** Action if $RPN \ge 100$ **OR** $S \ge 9$. - ✅ **New Method:** AIAG-VDA 2019 uses **Action Priority (AP)** = H/M/L instead of RPN. - ✅ **MTBF ↑ MTTR ↓** = higher Availability (TPM goal). - ✅ **FMEA Stages:** 10 stages from scope → action → re-score. - ✅ **KTU-Buzz Keywords:** *Nakajima, JIPM, OEE, Six Big Losses, RPN, Severity, Occurrence, Detection, Poka-Yoke, Autonomous Maintenance*. - ✅ **Integration Line:** "TPM *prevents* failures; FMEA *predicts* failures — together they deliver world-class OEE." > 🎯 **One-line Final Mantra for Exam Hall:** > *"TPM is the prevention engine; FMEA is the prediction radar; together they power Zero-Defect manufacturing."*

topics.title: ISO: Need for ISO 9000 and Other Quality Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT636 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ISO: Need for ISO 9000 and Other Quality Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is ISO?

The **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** is an independent, non-governmental international body that develops and publishes voluntary international standards to ensure the **quality, safety, efficiency, and interoperability** of products, services, and systems across virtually every industry. Founded in **Geneva, Switzerland in 23rd February 1947**, ISO brings together national standards bodies from over 167 countries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: The name "ISO" is derived from the Greek word *isos* meaning **"equal"** — reflecting the organization's mission to create uniform standards worldwide. ISO is **NOT** an acronym for "International Organization for Standardization".

### 1.2 What is ISO 9000?

The **ISO 9000 family** addresses various aspects of **Quality Management Systems (QMS)**. It provides a universally recognized framework for organizations to demonstrate their ability to consistently provide products and services that meet customer and regulatory requirements.

> [!NOTE]
> **ISO 9000:2015 Definition**: A *Quality Management System* is a set of coordinated activities to direct and control an organization with regard to quality. It is the formal system that documents policies, processes, procedures, and responsibilities for achieving quality objectives.

### 1.3 The Need for ISO 9000 — Intuitive Analogy

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Restaurant Rating System"**:
Imagine dining in a city where every restaurant cooks differently with no hygiene standards. One restaurant may be excellent, another terrible — and there's no way to know in advance. Now imagine a globally recognized star-rating system (like Michelin Stars) that independently certifies restaurants against uniform benchmarks of food quality, hygiene, and service. ISO 9000 plays **exactly this role** for businesses worldwide — it provides a **universal credibility certificate** that says, "This organization meets globally tested standards of quality, regardless of its country or industry."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why was ISO 9000 Needed?**
> - **Globalization of Trade**: Companies needed a common quality language across borders.
> - **Customer Confidence**: Buyers demanded proof of consistent quality.
> - **Reduction of Duplication**: Suppliers no longer needed multiple certifications for different customers.
> - **Legal & Contractual Requirements**: Many contracts (especially government and defense) began demanding ISO compliance.
> - **Rising Competition**: Quality became a key competitive differentiator.

### 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning | KTU Significance |
|------|---------|------------------|
| **ISO** | International body creating global standards | High-frequency 3-mark question |
| **QMS** | Quality Management System | Core concept of Module 4 |
| **ISO 9000** | Family of QMS standards | Most cited standard in TQM |
| **Certification** | Third-party audit confirming QMS compliance | Distinguishes from "Accreditation" |
| **Accreditation** | Approval given to certifying bodies | One level above certification |
| **Conformity** | Fulfillment of a requirement | Audit terminology |
| **Continuous Improvement** | Ongoing enhancement of QMS | TQM-Kaizen link |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (Often asked in KTU exams)**: *Certification* is issued to **organizations**, while *Accreditation* is issued to **certification bodies** that perform audits.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Evolution of ISO 9000 — A Historical Perspective

The ISO 9000 family has undergone several major revisions, each reflecting the evolution of quality management thought:

**Step-by-step evolution logic:**

- **1987 — First Publication**: ISO 9000 originated from the **BS 5750** (British Standard) and was based on military quality standards (MIL-Q-9858). It was heavily documentation-focused and applied primarily to manufacturing.

- **1994 Revision**: Introduced preventive actions and clarified existing requirements. Still heavily process-driven.

- **2000 Revision (Major Overhaul)**: Shifted from mere quality **assurance** to broader **management**. Introduced the **Eight Quality Management Principles** and the **Process Approach**.

- **2008 Revision**: Refinements for clarity and compatibility with ISO 14001 (Environmental Management).

- **2015 Revision (Current Version)**: Adopted the **High-Level Structure (HLS)** also called Annex SL, enabling seamless integration with other ISO management system standards. Introduced **Risk-Based Thinking** and reduced mandatory documentation.

### 2.2 The ISO 9000 Family — Structure

The ISO 9000 family consists of four core standards, each serving a distinct role:

| Standard | Full Title | Purpose |
|----------|-----------|---------|
